docs.title: Olden Era Companion
docs.text:
The complete strategy handbook for Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era — every spell, faction, hero archetype, skill, artifact, and creature, with verified numbers and worked battle math.

Start here: How to Use This Book · The Book at a Glance

Jump to: Foundations · Factions · Magic · Heroes · Decision Trees

Use the search bar (top) to find any spell, unit, or hero by name.

docs.title: How to Use This Book
docs.text: Heroes of Might and Magic: Olden Era rewards two kinds of knowledge: knowing what the numbers are, and knowing what they mean. The wiki has the first kind. This book exists for the second — and it never asks you to take the second kind on faith, because the first is always printed right next to it.

docs.title: The three-layer format
docs.text:
Every entry in this book — every spell, faction, skill, and artifact — follows the same shape:

  1. Reference. What the thing does, in plain English, with the exact formulas and verified numbers from official sources. No analysis, no opinion. If the source data has a gap, the entry says so instead of inventing something.
  2. Analysis. The math worked out: what the formula produces at real values, how it compares to its rivals, where it's strong, where it's weak, and the mistakes people actually make with it.
  3. Campaign Recommendation. A verdict — star rating, best users, and when to pick it — written for single-player campaign play unless an entry says otherwise.

docs.title: The reference army
docs.text: Combat math in the spell chapters is anchored to one consistent example so every number is comparable across the whole book: a stack of 20 Onyx Dancers — a real Dungeon Tier 3 ranged unit — with 18 HP each, ~120 damage per volley, 360 total stack HP, at Spellpower 10. When an entry says a spell "kills 7 dancers" or "saves a volley," that's the yardstick. Attack-vs-Defense modifiers are deliberately ignored in these examples (each point shifts damage ~5%; the full pipeline is in the Combat Mechanics appendix) so each spell's own contribution stays visible. Relative comparisons are exact; your absolute in-game numbers will differ.

docs.title: The rating key
docs.text:
Icon Meaning Scale ⭐ Overall Power 1–5 🟢 Beginner Friendly 1–5 ⚔️ Campaign Strength 1–5 💰 Economy Value 1–5 🧠 Skill Ceiling 1–5 📈 Early / Mid / Late Game three separate 1–5 scores ★★★☆☆ Campaign Verdict the entry's bottom line

A note on 🧠 Skill Ceiling: high is not praise and low is not an insult. A 1/5 means the thing works on autopilot; a 5/5 means its rating assumes you'll pilot it well — and warns you what it costs if you don't.

docs.title: Sourcing, honestly
docs.text:
Mechanics, formulas, stats, and costs come from the official Hooded Horse wiki and first-party stat documentation, with the retrieval date stamped in every chapter's footer and in each file's patch_verified field. Everything else — ratings, worked examples, synergies, builds, verdicts — is this book's original analysis and is labeled as such. Where sources conflict or a page couldn't be verified, the entry flags it rather than papering over it; the _audit.md files in the source tree track every known gap.

One warning worth repeating from nearly every chapter: the game is in Early Access. Numbers move. Every entry carries its verification date; when a balance patch lands, trust the patch notes over any number here until the entry's date catches up.

docs.title: A suggested reading order
docs.text:
• New to the game: the Combat Mechanics appendix first (ten minutes that make everything else legible), then the Temple faction chapter (the most forgiving faction, fully statted), then the Daylight Magic school.
  • Picking a faction: the six faction chapters end in comparable verdicts, and the Matchup Grid chapter shows how they collide.
  • Building a hero: Skills chapter → your faction's "recommended magic schools" section → the Artifacts build packages.
  • Stuck on a specific fight: the magic chapters' "In battle" sections are indexed to exactly that question — what a cast is actually worth against a real army.

docs.title: The Book at a Glance
docs.text: The table of contents that follows is generated from the chapters themselves; this page is the curated version — what each part is for, and how complete it currently is.

docs.title: Part I — Factions (six chapters + the Matchup Grid)
docs.text: One chapter per faction: identity, the faction-exclusive skill with verified mechanics, the unit roster with full stat tables, recommended magic schools cross-referenced to Part II's confirmed hero pairings, hero picks, three builds, and a verdict. Temple, Necropolis, Grove, Hive, and Dungeon carry complete verified stat tables; Schism carries its full roster tree and verified T5/T7 stats (its remaining rows are the book's one persistent sourcing gap — the official page resists retrieval). The Matchup Grid closes the part with all thirty pairings rated and the three structural rules they obey.

docs.title: Part II — Magic (five schools, 83 spells)
docs.text: The book's deepest section, and the one whose format everything else inherits. Every spell: plain English, the exact formula worked at all four levels, an "In battle" example against the reference army, the Masterful version and its owning hero where one exists, and a strategy layer with a verdict. Eleven Masterful hero pairings are confirmed from official sources, two near-confirmed, nine tracked as open. The Neutral Magic chapter also documents the level-gate and points systems unique to that school.

docs.title: Part III — Heroes, Skills, Artifacts, Creatures
docs.text:
• Skills: the full 30-skill taxonomy, the rank-and-subskill fork system, a complete worked Offense tree, and the synergy-clause build philosophy. The six faction skills live in their faction chapters; the four school skills in their magic chapters.
  • Artifacts: the 14-slot system, rarities, upgrading, set mechanics with both verified flagship sets, slot-by-slot standouts, and four assembled build packages.
  • Creatures: the neutral bestiary — 21 creatures across 8 tiers, acquisition rules, and how to fight (or recruit) the map's own army, up to the Tier-8 Lich Dragon.
  • Heroes: hero-class and per-hero coverage beyond the pairings already embedded in Parts I–II is the roadmap's next major pass.

docs.title: Appendices
docs.text: Combat Mechanics — the verified damage pipeline, morale/luck math, the creature-type table (including exactly what feeds Necromancy), and the documented stacking ceilings. The ten most load-bearing pages in the book; everything else's shorthand resolves here.

docs.title: What "complete" means in Early Access
docs.text: Every chapter footer carries a retrieval date, and every category's source folder carries an _audit.md ledger of verified content versus known gaps. This book's standing rule: a tracked gap over an invented number, every time. When the game leaves Early Access and the remaining wiki pages fill in, the audits are the checklist for the final edition.

docs.title: Foundations — how Olden Era actually works
docs.text: The chapters after this one assume you know what a "growth week" or a "Focus Charge" is. This one is where those assumptions are paid for. Everything here is grounded in the same verified sources as the rest of the book; where a foundational number hasn't been sourced yet, the entry says so.

docs.title: The four currencies of the game
docs.text:
1. Gold. Buys everything. Baseline income comes from towns and mines; the verified reference points scattered through this book give the scale: a T1 unit costs 35–115g, a T7 upgrade 2,000–5,000g, the Skull of Milos artifact prints +1,000g/day and is considered Legendary-tier for it.

2. Rare resources. Every faction's Tier 7 is gated behind one: Gems (Temple's Angels, Dungeon's Dragons), Mercury (Necropolis's Vampires), Crystal (Grove's Phoenixes, Hive's Queens). Verified pattern worth exploiting: each faction fields an economy-specialist hero for exactly its own bottleneck resource (Heretic Avis/Gems, Zam/Mercury, Mreowa/Crystals) — the game telling you what to hire a second hero for. A Marketplace converts between resources at a loss; the Schism build-order sourcing calls it the "conversion fallback," which is the right frame — a safety net, not a plan.

3. Time. The real currency. The game runs on a weekly rhythm: creature growth replenishes town dwellings every week (Skeletons at 30/week down to T7s at 1–2/week), Necromantic Energy refills weekly, and every economy effect in this book is priced per day. Movement points are the daily unit of time — which is why the Neutral Magic chapter rates the logistics suite (Town Portal, Second Wind, Relocation) so highly: they are literally time-generation spells.

4. Army. The only currency you fight with, and the one the factions disagree about most: Temple preserves it, Necropolis grows it from enemies, Grove pays premium gold for it, Hive spends it, Schism conjures it per-battle, Dungeon concentrates it. Reading a faction = knowing which conversion between the four currencies it's best at.

docs.title: The hero sheet
docs.text:
Four combat attributes, verified across the skills and hero sourcing: - Attack / Defense — feed the army-wide damage pipeline (each point ≈ 5% per the combat appendix). - Spellpower — scales spell formulas (the magic chapters' entire math axis) and durations. - Knowledge — the mana-side attribute (it also feeds the Avatar's stats directly, per the Neutral chapter). Exact mana-per-Knowledge formula: not yet sourced — tracked. - Plus Sight (scouting radius; the range multiplier for several map spells) and Movement (the daily budget; Second Wind's verified 40–60 restore gives the scale of a meaningful fraction).

Level-ups grant a random attribute point plus a three-way skill choice (fully documented in the Skills chapter). The compounding picks — Pip's +1 attribute per 2 levels, spell specialists' effective Spellpower bonuses — are the verified reason two heroes of equal level can be a tier apart in practice.

docs.title: How a battle flows
docs.text:
1. Tactics phase. Deploy within your placement zone — 3 lines by default, up to 5 with the Tactics skill, minus lines if the enemy out-Tactics you (the one directly adversarial skill).
  2. Rounds and turns. Units act in Initiative order; Wait defers. Morale grants extra turns (+4%/point), Luck grants bonus-damage strikes (+6%/point), native terrain grants +1 Initiative — all verified in the combat appendix, which also carries the full damage pipeline.
  3. The hero acts too. One spell per round (school-masters partially except themselves from the same-school lockout), plus Heroic Strike — the hero's personal attack, which an entire verified toolkit modifies (Old Lord Mandall's +damage specialty, Caduceus's mana refund, Two-faced Mask's kill-reset, Murmuring's Expert damage rider). Treat it as a small spell with no mana cost.
  4. Focus. Creatures earn Focus Points by attacking and being hit; banked points power their active abilities. The verified levers live in the Skills (Battle March), Magic (Energize, Silence), and Faction (Murmuring) chapters. Modern Olden Era combat runs on abilities — the book's recurring claim, and why Silence at 5 mana rates 4/5.
  5. Counterattacks are the hidden tax on melee — one per round by default, spent by the first attacker. Half the verified roster quirks in this book (Hydras' no-counter clause, Vampire Lord's Swift Strike, Dawn Hoplets, Long Reach as a mechanic) are ways of not paying it. Plan melee engagements around whose counter is spent.
  6. Sieges change the math entirely: walls, towers, and a gate force attackers through kill zones. The verified siege toolkit: Earth's Rage (the dedicated structure-nuke), Blink/flyers (over the walls), Siegecraft (the skill). The Matchup Grid's advice stands — the fights that decide campaigns are usually at a gate.

docs.title: The campaign arc (original analysis, grounded in the verified curves)
docs.text:
Week 1 is fight selection. Your army is small and losses are unaffordable; the verified intel tools (Read Minds, Groundsight, the map-spell suite) exist for this week. Every faction chapter's build-order guidance targets the same milestone: a functioning core by Week 2 (Arbitrators, Dread Knight chains, Lightweavers via Zenith).

Weeks 2–4 are the snowball window. Growth compounds: each week's recruits, each cleared mine, each leveled skill multiplies the next. This is where the clock-based matchups from the grid are decided — tempo factions must convert their early edge before the inevitability factions' curves cross theirs.

Week 5+ belongs to whoever converted best. T7s come online (rare-resource permitting), set bonuses and school-masters hit full scaling, and single battles start deciding maps. The verified late-game power spikes — Judgement's 900, Chain Lightning's chains, Armageddon builds, the morale-stack ceiling — all live here.

The one rule underneath all of it: every system in this game converts into every other (gold→army→XP→skills→bigger fights→more gold), and the factions are just different exchange rates. Play the conversion, not the turn.

docs.title: Open gaps (tracked)
docs.text:
Exact mana/Knowledge and XP-curve formulas; movement-point base values per hero type; AI behavior documentation (the original brief's fourth foundations topic — no reliable sourcing exists yet for Early Access AI patterns, and this book won't invent them); weekly event types (plague/double-growth weeks etc.) pending a dedicated fetch.

Sourcing note: every specific number above is cross-referenced from this book's verified chapters (combat appendix, faction stat tables, skills, magic, heroes); the campaign-arc framing and the four-currencies model are original analysis. Gaps are enumerated rather than filled with folklore.

docs.title: The Matchup Grid — every faction vs. every faction
docs.text:
How to read this: each cell is written from the row faction's perspective, grounded in the verified stat tables and skill mechanics in the six faction chapters and the spell data in the magic chapters. Matchups describe structural pressure — who must act, who profits from time — not guaranteed outcomes; pilots beat pairings. Ratings: F (favored), E (even), U (unfavored).

vs → Temple Necropolis Grove Hive Schism Dungeon Temple — E E F F E Necropolis E — F F E E Grove E U — E F E Hive U U E — E E Schism U E U E — E Dungeon E E E E E —

docs.title: The nine matchup families (row vs. column)
docs.text:
Temple vs. Necropolis (E): buffs against inevitability. Temple's per-fight edge is real — Radiant Armour + Marksmen out-trade skeleton volleys — but Necromancy converts every fight anywhere on the map into Necropolis growth. Temple's win condition is tempo it doesn't naturally have; its equalizer is that four of its Clerics carry elite Masterful spells, and one 4-mana Fatal Decay (learnable cross-school) shuts off Vampirism in the fights that matter. The clock favors the dead; the battles favor the sun. Even.

Temple vs. Hive (F): the wall against the wave. Hive's entire gameplan — Stinger Initiative 12 alpha strikes, Larva screens — runs into the one roster built to receive it: Sun's Aegis 8-Def T1s, Guardian Griffins, Radiant Armour L4 (-35% army-wide). Every blunted first wave hands Righteousness stacks to the Temple hero. Hive must hit harder than Temple can armor, and the math (Hive's fragile HP pools vs. Temple's fixed-damage Lightweavers) says it usually can't.

Temple vs. Schism (F): Temple owns the two cleanest anti-Schism tools in the game — Heavenly Blades L4 (deletes summoned units on contact: Communion reinforcements, Starchildren, all of it) and a roster durable enough to survive the streak-fueled opening. Break one fight and Communion halves itself into irrelevance overnight.

Necropolis vs. Grove (F): free army against the most expensive army in the game. Every Grove stack Necropolis kills costs real gold to replace; every Necropolis stack Grove kills feeds nothing (undead don't fear attrition) and returns via Rewind Death/Vampirism anyway. Grove's out is Faleor/Halon ending fights before the grind starts — possible, but the structural pressure is one-directional.

Necropolis vs. Hive (F): the immunity matchup. Hive's corpse economy meets a faction whose dead don't feed Necromancy's enemies — wait, reverse it: Hive Spawns DO feed Necromancy (verified type-interaction), meaning every dead swarm unit is a future Necropolis recruit, while Necropolis's Undead feed Hive's consumption abilities nothing they can't get elsewhere. Add Sleep-immunity symmetry (both sides' control fizzles) and the fight reduces to raw attrition — the one game Necropolis never loses.

Grove vs. Schism (F): Grove's Focus economy (Murmuring's free battle-start charges) comes online turn one, faster than Communion's streak-building, and Faleor's turn-one Masterful Fireball erases the fragile Schism early roster the moment it clusters around its summons. Schism's Envoy-Armageddon endgame is real, but Grove's Phoenix-Initiative endgame arrives first.

Hive vs. Temple / Necropolis (U, U): covered above from the other side — the swarm's two structural nightmares: the roster that armors through the alpha strike, and the roster that recruits the swarm's corpses.

Schism vs. Temple / Grove (U, U): the anti-summon problem in two flavors: Temple's Heavenly Blades and Grove's tempo casters. Schism's best games are against factions without a summon answer.

Dungeon vs. everyone (E across the board): the verified roster explains the row: no bad stats, no hard counters against it, and its identity (Luck lines, no-counter Hydras, penalty-free Medusas, the fastest T7 in the game carrying spell immunity) is flexibility — Dungeon always has a game plan, and never a free win. Its matchups are decided by the pilot's stance-skill timing and whether the Black Dragon/Armageddon package comes online before the opponent's own curve peaks. The one asymmetry worth naming: vs. Necropolis and Schism, Dungeon's tempo must beat their inevitability clocks — win by week 4–5 or the grinding factions grind.

docs.title: Three cross-cutting rules the grid keeps repeating
docs.text:
1. Sustain factions beat tempo factions that hesitate — Necropolis and Schism convert time into army; against them, slow play is a resource transfer.
  2. Anti-summon tech warps three matchups — Heavenly Blades L4 and Anti-Magic swing every Schism pairing and dent Hive's Larva game; check the enemy faction before skipping those entries.
  3. Type-immunity fine print decides mirror-adjacent fights — Sleep's exclusion list (Undead, Hive Spawn, Embodiment, Construct) and Necromancy's reanimation table (Living/Hive Spawn/Magic Creature/Dragon feed it; Undead/Embodiment/Construct don't) are worth memorizing; half this grid's edges come from them.

Sourcing note: structural claims grounded in the verified faction stat tables, skill mechanics, and type-interaction rules documented in this book (all retrieved from official sources, 2026-07-03). The grid's ratings are original analysis and the least stable content in the book — re-check after every balance patch.

docs.title: Dungeon
docs.text: ⭐ Overall Power: 5/5 · 🟢 Beginner Friendly: 3/5 · ⚔️ Campaign Strength: 4/5 💰 Economy Value: 3/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 4/5 📈 Early: 4/5 · Mid: 4/5 · Late: 5/5

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
(What the wiki says, translated into plain English — no opinions yet.)

  • Governing body: The Triumvirate of Alvar — formerly an underground power, now operating as the backstage rulers of Jadame.
  • Identity / theme: Dark elves, beasts, and dragons. The faction's signature trait is flexibility — nearly every unit has two distinct attack types rather than one fixed role.
  • Faction skill — Triumvirate's Strength: every Dungeon hero starts with this skill, and no other class can learn it. Once per turn, before or during combat, the hero picks one of three stances that buffs the whole army for that fight. The stance stays active until you switch it, so it also functions as a between-battle mode toggle:
  • Minotaur Stance → boosts Attack
  • Dark Elf Stance → boosts Defense
  • Dragon Stance → boosts Spell Power
  • Native terrain: Underground (subterranean maps).
  • Key resource dependency: Gems. Dungeon's Tier-VII creature dwelling (Cave Palace, home of Dragons) requires Gems on top of Gold, and hiring the Dragons themselves also costs Gems, not just Gold. Ore is a secondary early bottleneck for mid-tier dwellings.
  • Unit roster (T1–T7), with upgrade paths:
Tier Base unit Upgrade A Upgrade B T1 (scout/cheap unit) T2 Infiltrator Guile Infiltrator Bleak Infiltrator T3 Dancer Onyx Dancer Jasper Dancer / Aureate Dancer* T4 Minotaur Minotaur Lord Minotaur Vanguard T5 Medusa Medusa Sculptor Medusa Queen T6 Hydra Chthonic Hydra Infernal Hydra T7 Cave Dragon Black Dragon Ashen Dragon

*Community sourcing lists both Jasper and Aureate Dancer as upgrade names in different builds — verify against the current patch before treating this row as final; flagged for the next audit pass.

  • Hero classes: Dungeon fields both a Might class and a Magic class.
  • Overlord (Might): like all Might heroes, cannot learn Thaumaturgy — leans on tactics, brute force, and the stance system instead of spells.

docs.title: Analysis
docs.text: (Why it matters — this is the original content, not wiki restatement.)

docs.title: Strengths
docs.text:
• Best-in-game clear speed. Onyx Dancers give Dungeon the fastest, most efficient early-game ranged clearing of any of the six factions — the single biggest reason it's widely rated the strongest faction at launch.
  • Dual-attack flexibility. Because most units carry two attack types, Dungeon armies adapt to a wider range of enemy compositions than single-role rosters.
  • Stance system gives free, repeatable tactical flexibility — you're never locked into one buff profile for the whole game the way a fixed passive would lock you.
  • Best late-game payoff in the game. A Black Dragon stack backed by upgraded ranged units is considered one of the single strongest late-game army configurations across all factions.

docs.title: Weaknesses
docs.text:
• Gem-gated Tier VII means the strongest unit in the roster is the most resource-fragile — a Gem-poor map materially delays your win condition.
  • Early roster (Infiltrators) is fragile and easy to over-invest in if you're new to the faction; it looks like a core unit and isn't.
  • Rewards map knowledge and resource-route planning more than factions with flatter power curves — part of why it's rated only middling on beginner-friendliness despite being top-tier in raw power.

docs.title: Economic priorities
docs.text:
1. Ore first — gates several mid-tier dwellings and is the first hard bottleneck.
  2. Gems second — becomes critical once you're building toward the Tier VII Dragon dwelling; claim Gem outposts as soon as the mid-game building path is secure.
  3. Gold requirement for the Week 1 build is comparatively low, which is what makes the early rare-resource demands manageable if you scout for them deliberately.

docs.title: Army composition
docs.text:
• Early: Onyx Dancers as the clearing core, a small Infiltrator stack only for cheap chip damage — not a real investment.
  • Mid: Minotaurs added as the frontline screen (their job is to absorb hits, not to out-damage the ranged core — don't judge them by raw damage), Medusae added as a second ranged/control layer once mid-tier buildings are up.
  • Late: Black Dragons as the win condition, layered behind upgraded ranged units (Toxic Troglodytes' long-range attack is worth keeping active specifically because it avoids provoking retaliation).

docs.title: Best hero archetypes
docs.text:
• Overlord (Might): stance-driven tactical play, front-loads Attack or Defense depending on matchup; no Thaumaturgy access, so army quality and stance timing carry the hero rather than spellcasting.
  • (Magic-class Dungeon hero entry pending audit — wiki coverage for the Dungeon spellcaster class was incomplete at time of writing; see _audit.md.)

docs.title: Recommended magic schools
docs.text:
(Filled from the completed magic-category chapters, 2026-07-03.)

Primary: Primal — for one legendary build and one great hero fit. The Black Dragon + Armageddon package (see the Primal chapter's Armageddon entry, ★★★★) is Dungeon's signature magic play: your spell-immune T7 shrugs off the map-wide 500-per-stack blast that deletes everything else. Even before dragons, Primal's damage suite fits Triumvirate's Strength perfectly — swap to the Spellpower stance the round you cast Chain Lightning and the stance bonus lands exactly where it counts.

Secondary: Daylight, one spell — Arina's Chosen. Dungeon owns its Masterful version: Sister Deira (Warlock) starts with "Mask of the Sun" (Masterful Arina's Chosen: the death-ward plus +1 action point, +1 effective SP per 3 hero levels — see the Daylight chapter). On a faction whose T7 costs Gems and whose elite stacks are genuinely irreplaceable, the game's best stack-insurance spell in native hands is worth building a second hero around.

Tech notes: Nightshade's Web protects the Onyx/Aureate Dancer core from melee divers (the Dancers' melee attack is -50%, so anything that reaches them halves your damage); and remember your own Black Dragons' spell immunity blocks your buffs too — Armageddon builds fly clean, buff builds and dragons don't mix.

docs.title: Recommended magic-class hero
docs.text: • Sister Deira (Warlock): Masterful Arina's Chosen, per her official wiki page — the Dungeon spellcaster entry previously pending audit, now confirmed. (Tellaris the Betrayed is the faction's other confirmed spell specialist per official patch notes; exact spell pending the hero-verification pass.)

docs.title: Verified full roster (data-fill pass, 2026-07-03)
docs.text:
The official Dungeon Units table corrects and completes this chapter's original walkthrough: the roster begins with a Troglodyte line the draft missed, shifting the true tier ladder to Troglodyte (T1), Infiltrator (T2), Dancer (T3), Minotaur (T4), Medusa (T5), Hydra (T6), Cave Dragon (T7). Two reads the numbers add to the chapter's analysis: the whole roster runs on Luck/no-counter/penalty-free clauses rather than raw stat bloat — the "two attack types / versatility" identity in table form — and Black Dragon's Initiative 14 ties Grove's base Phoenix for the fastest-acting T7 body in the game, which is exactly what an Armageddon carrier wants.

docs.title: First-week build order
docs.text:
Day Action Why 1 Build the Amphitheater (base Onyx Dancer dwelling); recruit everything available Starts weekly unit growth immediately — the earlier this is up, the more Dancers you have by Week 2 2 Upgrade the town hall (Byzantine Palace) to Tier 2 Increases Gold and Law Point income; income compounds, so this is worth doing before combat buildings 3 Upgrade the town hall to Tier 3 Expensive, but the same compounding logic applies — later is strictly worse 4 Build the Labyrinth (Minotaur dwelling) Unlocks your frontline screening unit before you need to fight anything that punishes a pure-ranged army 5–7 Flexible: Bank (if Gold-stable) / Marketplace (if Gems or Crystals are short) / Mage Guild (if your hero needs spells to clear safely) Read your specific map's resource shortages rather than following a fixed script here

Target state entering Week 2: town hall fully upgraded, both core dwellings (Amphitheater + Labyrinth) active, enough rare resources banked to push toward the Amphitheater's Dancer upgrade and the mid-game building path.

docs.title: The Three Builds
docs.text:
1. Dancer Rush — lean into the T1-3 ranged clear speed, prioritize Amphitheater upgrades over everything else, aim to out-tempo the opponent before Tier VI/VII armies exist on either side. Best on maps with easy early Gold and weak neutral guards.
  2. Ore-into-Gems Standard Build — the default 90%-of-games plan above: Ore first for the mid-game spine, Gem outposts claimed as soon as they're safe to reach, Black Dragons as the explicit long-game target from Day 1 planning.
  3. Stance-Flex War Hero — built around an Overlord who swaps stances turn-to-turn rather than committing to one; strongest in multiplayer/PvP-leaning campaign scenarios where you can't predict the next fight's shape and the flexibility outweighs a slightly slower power curve.

docs.title: Common beginner mistakes
docs.text:
• Over-recruiting Infiltrators because they're cheap and available Day 1 — Gold spent here should almost always go to Minotaurs or Medusae once those buildings exist.
  • Treating Minotaurs as underwhelming because their damage looks low on paper — their job is holding the frontline so Dancers don't eat retaliation, not winning the damage race themselves.
  • Ignoring Gem outposts until the Cave Palace is already queued, which pushes the Dragon timeline later than it needed to be.

docs.title: Advanced tactics
docs.text:
• Route scouting explicitly around Gem nodes in the mid-game rather than general exploration — on Gem-poor maps, plan the Dragon timeline to slip and compensate with a heavier upgraded-ranged-unit late game instead of forcing the Dragon spike.
  • Use the stance system as a mid-combat read, not just a pre-battle choice — switching from Dark Elf (Defense) to Minotaur (Attack) once a fight is clearly won can accelerate the kill and reduce turns taken, which matters for chained fights in the same day.

docs.title: Campaign Verdict: ★★★★★ (5/5)
docs.text:
Dungeon combines the fastest early clear speed in the game with what is widely considered the single best late-game army composition (upgraded ranged + Black Dragons), at the cost of a Gem dependency that punishes poor map knowledge and a fragile early roster that's easy to over-invest in as a new player. For campaign (single-player) play specifically, it's rated well-suited to Single Hero mode, which further plays to its clear-speed strength.

Related: Overlord (Hero Class) · Onyx Dancer · Minotaur · Black Dragon · Dungeon Laws · Dungeon Units — full roster

Sourcing note: built from the official Hooded Horse wiki (Dungeon, Dungeon Units, Overlord, Minotaur, Chthonic Hydra, Cave Dragon, Infiltrator, Guile Infiltrator, Bleak Infiltrator pages, retrieved 2026-07-03) plus cross-referenced third-party strategy coverage for the Analysis and Campaign Recommendation layers, which are original synthesis, not wiki content. Sections marked STUB are intentionally left unfilled rather than guessed — see _audit.md for what's still needed.

docs.title: Grove — The Murmurwoods
docs.text:
⭐ Power: 4/5 · 🟢 Beginner Friendly: 4/5 · ⚔️ Campaign: 4/5 · 💰 Economy: 3/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 3/5 · 📈 Early 3/5 · Mid 4/5 · Late 5/5

In one sentence: The magic faction — elemental spirits that are each "a unique powerhouse," the safest ranged roster in the game for newer players, and the deepest bench of elemental-damage hero specialists anywhere, at the price of the most expensive units.

Identity (per the official faction page): the Murmurwoods' governing body is officially Unknown; elementals & magical creatures; keywords Strong Spellcasters and Creature Abilities. Community assessments consistently call it the safest, most beginner-friendly ranged roster — with "strong and expensive" as the official description of its rangers and druids, and that cost is the faction's real constraint.

docs.title: Faction skill: Murmuring
docs.text:
Known by default by all Wardens and Druids (Grove's two hero classes), unavailable elsewhere.

What it does (official mechanics, verified): the hero starts every battle with Focus Charges already banked — 1 at Basic, 2 at Advanced, 3 at Expert. Focus is the resource creatures spend on their active abilities, so this skill means Grove's ability-rich roster (and its hero) opens fights with its best tricks already loaded instead of grinding attacks to earn them.

Documented subskills: Mycelium Grounds (+10% dealt / -10% taken on Autumn terrain), Life Essence (+1 Focus Charge cap), Child of the Woods (+20 max mana); at Expert: Just One More Time (every Focus spend cuts all the hero's spell cooldowns by 2 rounds), Burst of Energy (Focus spends add +15% per hero level to Heroic Strike damage), and Strong Connection (each Focus spend gives the hero +2 to all attributes until end of round).

Why it fits: the Expert tree turns Focus spending into a hero-stat engine — and note the direct synergy with Arcane's Energize/Energy Explosion package (see that chapter): Grove is the one faction whose faction skill feeds the Focus-economy combo natively.

docs.title: Unit roster (full stats, verified against the official wiki)
docs.text: Native terrain: Autumn.

docs.title: Tier 1: Faun → Faun Archer / Faun Warrior
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Notes Faun 115g 11 4/3 3–5 4 2 Ranged, no melee penalty Faun Archer 150g 11 4/3 3–5 5 3 Ranged, NO penalties at all Faun Warrior 150g 11 6/6 4–6 6 4 Melee

A T1 shooter with no melee penalty even unupgraded — the safest starter unit in the game, exactly as the faction's reputation says. Faun Archer removes every ranged penalty (distance and melee) on a 150-gold unit, which is absurd; Faun Warrior exists for players who somehow want Grove without shooters.

docs.title: Tier 2: Hoplet → Dawn Hoplet / Dusk Hoplet
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Morale/Luck Init Spd Notes Hoplet 150g 18 5/3 3–6 1/1 6 5 Melee Dawn Hoplet 200g 18 7/3 3–6 1/2 6 6 Melee, no counter Dusk Hoplet 200g 18 5/5 3–6 2/1 9 5 Melee

The only Grove line with native Morale and Luck — the faction's "lucky" flavor made mechanical. Dawn Hoplet attacks without ever being counterattacked (a free Shackles built into a T2!); Dusk Hoplet at Initiative 9 is the early tempo screen.

docs.title: Tier 3: Vine Iriyad → Fungal Iriyad / Crystal Iriyad
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Vine Iriyad 235g 45 6/11 4–8 2 3 Fungal Iriyad 320g 45 6/11 4–8 3 4 Crystal Iriyad 320g 45 6/11 5–7 3 5

The wall: 45 HP at Tier 3 (compare the Onyx Dancer's 18) with 11 Defense — nearly triple the durability of peer-tier units, moving at a glacier's pace. This is the shield the Fauns and Naiads shoot from behind. Upgrade differences are marginal; take whichever resource curve suits and don't overthink it.

docs.title: Tier 4: Naiad → Vernal Naiad / Brumal Naiad
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Notes Naiad 380g 40 11/10 10–14 5 5 Ranged Vernal Naiad 530g 40 11/14 14–18 7 6 Ranged Brumal Naiad 530g 40 15/10 14–18 6 5 Ranged

The mid-game ranged core (the spring/winter fork the naming telegraphed). Vernal (spring): +4 Defense and Initiative 7 — survivability and tempo. Brumal (winter): +4 Attack — pure output. Vernal is the default; the Iriyad wall keeps Brumal safe enough if you prefer damage.

docs.title: Tier 5: Herbomancer → Sporemancer / Murmurmancer
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Notes Herbomancer 640g 60 12/14 15–18 6 4 Long Reach Sporemancer 910g 60 16/18 15–18 8 6 Long Reach Murmurmancer 910g 60 12/14 18–18 fixed 8 5 Long Reach

The druids of the official faction blurb. Note Murmurmancer's fixed 18–18 damage — zero variance, named for the faction skill itself. Sporemancer is the stat pick (+4/+4); Murmurmancer the consistency pick.

docs.title: Tier 6: Qilin → Thunder Qilin / Mist Qilin
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Luck Init Spd Qilin 1300g 135 22/22 24–30 3 9 6 Thunder Qilin 2000g 135 22/22 30–36 3 11 8 Mist Qilin 2000g 135 24/24 27–36 3 13 7

Native Luck 3 on the whole line — these spirits crit constantly. Mist Qilin at Initiative 13 acts before nearly everything in the game; Thunder hits harder per swing. Both are elite; the fork is tempo vs. throughput.

docs.title: Tier 7: Phoenix → Flaming Phoenix / Energy Phoenix
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Phoenix 2800g + 1 Crystal 200 28/26 45–65 14 7 Flaming Phoenix 4700g + 2 Crystal 300 34/26 45–65 16 9 Energy Phoenix 4700g + 2 Crystal 250 28/36 45–65 15 9

The fastest-acting T7 line in the game — base Phoenix's Initiative 14 already beats every other faction's upgraded T7, and Flaming Phoenix's 16 is the highest Initiative printed on any unit in this book. Grove's whole tempo problem (slow walls, Initiative-2 Iriyads) inverts at the top of the roster. Crystal is the rare-resource bottleneck. Flaming for the stats; Energy for the armor.

docs.title: Recommended magic schools (cross-referenced)
docs.text:
Primary: Primal — Grove owns this school at the hero level. Both of Primal's confirmed Masterful specialists are Grove heroes: Faleor (Masterful Fireball — bigger area; the pick community guides call Grove's best hero outright) and Halon (Masterful Chain Lightning — half damage loss per jump, pushing chain totals toward ~1,800). The logic is airtight for the faction's problem profile: expensive units mean every fight should be won before contact, and turn-one multi-stack AoE is exactly that. Read those two entries in the Primal chapter first.

Primary co-pick: Arcane. Vatawna's Masterful Mirror Copy (clones enemy stacks to fight for you) is Grove's third confirmed Masterful hero — and Rewind Life is even more valuable here than elsewhere because of unit prices: reviving a wiped Grove stack refunds more gold than reviving anyone else's.

The takeaway: Grove has three confirmed Masterful heroes across two schools — more offensive-magic specialist depth than any other faction — plus the longest spell-specialist patch-notes cohort in the game (Old Pilgrim, Aeliniel, Glacia, Vim, Echolily, Sullie alongside the three above). If you want to play the game as a wizard with an army attached rather than the reverse, this is the faction.

docs.title: Hero picks
docs.text:
• Faleor (the default): Masterful Fireball from level 1 solves the fragile-expensive-early-game problem by killing threats before they arrive. Best first pick.
  • Halon (the late-game ceiling): Masterful Chain Lightning; the single highest sustained-nuke output in the game once leveled.
  • Vatawna (the trickster): Masterful Mirror Copy targeting enemy stacks — steals the enemy's best unit as a battle plan.
  • Aunt Daliar (the might route): Warden (Grove's Might class), Insight specialist — the pick for players running Grove's army as the main act rather than the supporting cast.
  • Alluring Sh'a: diplomacy specialist — converting neutral armies to your side sidesteps the faction's gold-cost problem entirely, a quietly perfect thematic fit.

docs.title: Three builds
docs.text:
1. The Patient Forest (beginner): Faun Archers + ranged core behind Hoplet screens; Faleor clearing approaches with Fireball. The "safest ranged roster" reputation, played straight.

2. Stormcaller (mid/late): Halon + maximum Spellpower investment; army exists to hold the line while Chain Lightning does the actual killing. Grove as a delivery system for one spell.

3. The Mirrorwood (advanced): Vatawna stealing enemy elites + Alluring Sh'a's diplomacy feeding converted neutrals into the army — Grove's gold problem solved by never paying for units at all.

docs.title: Matchups (vs. completed chapters)
docs.text: Against Dungeon: a shooters-vs-shooters race where Dungeon's Initiative edge (Aureate Dancers) usually fires first — Grove's answer is Faleor deleting Dancer stacks in the approach. Against Temple: mirror-adjacent (both buff-and-shoot factions); Grove's AoE specialists punish Temple's tight defensive formations, while Temple's Wean-resistant... rather, Temple's cleanse suite (Inner Light) strips Grove's setup. Against Necropolis: your expensive-unit economy vs. their free one — end it early or don't. Full matchup grid: STUB.

docs.title: Common mistakes
docs.text:
• Playing Grove like a might faction and bleeding gold on replacement units — the entire design points at spells-first play.
  • Picking a generalist hero over Faleor/Halon/Vatawna and forfeiting the faction's defining advantage.
  • Fighting attrition wars against Necropolis with the most expensive units in the game.
  • Ignoring diplomacy tools that exist precisely to offset unit costs.

docs.title: Verdict: ★★★★☆ (4/5)
docs.text:
The wizard's faction, and the best expression of magic-forward play in the game — three Masterful specialists across the two best offensive schools, on top of a safe ranged roster. One star withheld for the economy: everything Grove fields is priced like it's irreplaceable, and against fast tempo or free-growth factions, sometimes it is.

Sourcing note: faction identity from the official Factions page; roster tree from the official Units navigation; hero specializations from the magic chapters' confirmed pairings and official patch notes (retrieved 2026-07-03). Murmuring skill text and full unit stat tables are open gaps logged in the factions audit. Builds, ratings, and verdicts are original analysis.

docs.title: Hive — The Order of the Fly
docs.text:
⭐ Power: 3/5 · 🟢 Beginner Friendly: 2/5 · ⚔️ Campaign: 3/5 · 💰 Economy: 3/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 5/5 · 📈 Early 4/5 · Mid 3/5 · Late 3/5

In one sentence: The swarm-aggression faction — the Dragonfly King's insect legions overwhelm through speed, numbers, and ability-driven units, rewarding players who attack constantly and punishing everyone who hesitates.

Identity (per the official faction page): Beelzebub's Order of the Fly, commanded by the Dragonfly King, "bent on conquering Jadame" and "driven by swift and aggressive tactics." Community tier assessments consistently place Hive among the factions "requiring much higher mechanical skill and specific conditions to reach full potential" — this is the expert's aggression faction, not the beginner's.

docs.title: Faction skill: Summon Swarm
docs.text:
The Hive-exclusive skill (all Hive heroes start with some rank of it — e.g. Mila begins with Basic Summon Swarm per her official page).

What it does (official mechanics, verified): once per round, the hero lays a clutch of eggs on the battlefield; at the start of the next round they hatch into a stack of Fire Larvae whose total HP equals a percentage of the combined HP of all Hive Spawns in the hero's army — 8% at Basic, 12% at Advanced, 16% at Expert — with ability strength scaling with hero level. The eggs are attackable (destroyed after two hits), adding a mind-game: enemies must waste attacks on eggs or accept the hatch.

The math that matters: the summon scales with your army's total HP, so it grows all game — a late-game army with 3,000 combined Hive HP hatches a 480-HP larva stack at Expert, every round, forever. Free bodies as a percentage of your bodies: the swarm that spawns swarms. This also confirms the roster's structural tell — Fire Larva sits outside the upgrade system entirely because it's the skill's output, not a recruited unit.

Class note — the Herald (Hive's Magic class): officially documented subclasses are Progenitor (+200% creature growth in all your cities — on the swarm faction, an obscene multiplier) and Lord of Chaos (Heroic Strike deals +2 damage per hero attribute point). Progenitor is the campaign-defining one: it's the biggest growth multiplier printed anywhere in the game.

docs.title: Unit roster (full stats, verified against the official wiki)
docs.text: Native terrain: Lava.

docs.title: The swarm unit: Fire Larva (no upgrades)
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Fire Larva 100g 8 4/4 4–4 fixed 4 4

Officially listed at Tier IV with no upgrade paths — the roster's structural confirmation of its swarm-fodder role (and of the Summon Swarm skill's output). 8 HP for 100 gold is terrible value as an army unit and perfect value as ammunition: fixed 4 damage, a body on a hex, and nothing worth mourning.

docs.title: Tier 1: Parasite → Warden Parasite / Ravager Parasite
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Notes Parasite 90g 7 3/3 2–4 4 4 Long Reach Warden Parasite 110g 9 3/6 2–4 5 5 Long Reach Ravager Parasite 110g 7 6/3 2–4 6 6 Long Reach

A Long Reach T1 — hits from a hex away without eating counterattacks, the aggression identity in miniature. Ravager (Init/Spd 6/6) for the tempo swarm; Warden to actually hold ground.

docs.title: Tier 2: Locust → Overgrown Locust / Harvester Locust
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Locust 130g 12 5/4 3–5 6 5 Overgrown Locust 170g 16 5/4 3–5 7 6 Harvester Locust 170g 12 7/4 4–5 7 7

Fast, cheap, numerous — the swarm's rank and file. Harvester at Speed 7 crosses most battlefields turn one, which is the entire Hive gameplan in one stat.

docs.title: Tier 3: Hornet → Chanter / Stinger
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Hornet 255g 20 7/10 4–9 10 7 Chanter 350g 25 7/10 4–9 4 11 Stinger 350g 20 7/10 4–9 12 9

The core stack (hero Leira's specialty line). Base Hornet already acts at Initiative 10; Stinger pushes to 12/9 — first-strike melee that reaches any backline turn one. Chanter is the oddity: Initiative 4 but Speed 11, an ability-oriented support flyer that moves huge distances late in the round. Stinger is the aggression default.

docs.title: Tier 4: Scorpion → Volcanic Scorpion / Spelaean Scorpion
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Morale/Luck Init Spd Scorpion 280g 45 12/12 10–12 1/1 3 4 Volcanic Scorpion 390g 60 12/14 10–12 1/2 4 5 Spelaean Scorpion 390g 45 14/12 12–14 2/1 4 4

The swarm's one true tank line — and the Morale/Luck carrier. Slow (Init 3–4) by design: it holds the line the fast units flow around. Volcanic (60 HP) is the anvil; Spelaean trades bulk for bite.

docs.title: Tier 5: Reaver → Menacing Reaver / Maniacal Reaver
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Morale Init Spd Reaver 770g 45 18/12 10–40 1 11 6 Menacing Reaver 1100g 45 18/18 20–30 3 12 7 Maniacal Reaver 1100g 45 18/12 10–40 2 12 6

The gambler's unit: base damage range 10–40 is the widest variance in the game — a 20-stack swings anywhere from 200 to 800. Menacing narrows it to 20–30 (higher floor, Morale 3); Maniacal keeps the chaos. Pair Maniacal with max-damage effects (Kennelmaster-style marks, Masterful Blessing logic) and the variance becomes a weapon; otherwise Menacing is the adult choice.

docs.title: Tier 6: Waurms → Devourer / Pyroboros
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Luck Init Spd Notes Waurms 1200g 120 20/20 21–23 1 8 8 Melee Devourer 1900g 120 20/20 24–26 3 8 10 Melee Pyroboros 1900g 120 20/20 21–23 2 9 8 Ranged, no melee penalty

Pyroboros is the roster's plot twist: the melee swarm faction's T6 upgrades into a full shooter with no melee penalty — a genuine ranged core piece hiding at the top of the tree. Devourer (Speed 10, Luck 3) is the pursuit predator for the classic all-in build.

docs.title: Tier 7: Hive Queen → Hive Mother / Hive Huntress
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Notes Hive Queen 2100g + 1 Crystal 200 25/25 60–60 fixed 8 6 Melee Hive Mother 3500g + 2 Crystal 275 25/25 60–60 13 6 Melee Hive Huntress 3500g + 2 Crystal 250 25/25 70–70 8 9 Melee, no counter

Fixed damage across the whole line — the Queen always hits for exactly 60 (the patch-notes attention this line received makes sense; there's no bad roll to soften her). Hive Mother (275 HP, Initiative 13) is the royal anchor; Huntress (70 fixed, never counterattacked, Speed 9) is a guided missile. Crystal-gated like Grove's Phoenix.

docs.title: Recommended magic schools (cross-referenced)
docs.text:
Primary: Daylight — one spell above all: Haste, because Hive owns its Masterful version. Mila (Herald) starts with Masterful Haste — army-wide speed, +1 effective Spellpower per 2 levels — on the faction whose official identity is "swift and aggressive tactics." It's the single cleanest faction/spell fit in the game: every point of Speed converts directly into earlier contact, and earlier contact is the whole Hive thesis. (Mila also starts with Basic Daylight Magic, per her official page — the faction's magic class is genuinely built for this school.)

Primary co-pick: Arcane's Focus package. The faction keyword Creature Abilities + ability-driven units = Energize and Energy Explosion (see the Arcane chapter) are worth more here than to any other faction: front-loading Focus charges means your units' actives fire on round one, matching the tempo plan.

Situational: Nightshade's Silence is the mirror-match terror — an enemy silencing your ability-dependent army removes most of what makes it dangerous; know the threat even if you never cast it.

docs.title: Hero picks
docs.text:
• Mila (the flagship): Masterful Haste on the speed faction. The obvious pick is also the correct one. Her lore (a Church deserter answered by the Dragonfly King) even makes her the thematic bridge from Temple's buff school to Hive's aggression.
  • Leira (the creature route): Hornets specialist — the pick when you'd rather scale the core stack than the tempo.
  • Any Herald → Progenitor: the +200% growth subclass is a campaign plan by itself: chase its five prerequisite skills and the swarm becomes a flood.
  • Niev: magic-damage specialist (per official patch notes) for a more caster-forward Hive.

docs.title: Three builds
docs.text:
1. The First Wave (tempo): Mila + Masterful Haste + Hornet core + Fire Larva screens. Contact on turn one, everywhere, always. The faction played as designed.

2. Progenitor's Flood (campaign): any Herald raced to the Progenitor subclass; +200% growth turns the numbers advantage into something no economy matches. Slower to come online; unanswerable after.

3. Focus Storm (advanced): Energize/Energy Explosion package + ability-heavy units (Scorpions/Reavers) — every fight opens with a full ability salvo. The highest-skill expression of the roster.

docs.title: Matchups (vs. completed chapters)
docs.text: Against Dungeon: the tempo mirror — Aureate Dancer initiative vs. Masterful Haste; whoever lands the first real volley usually wins the war. Against Temple: their wall (Sun's Aegis/Radiant Armour) exists to blunt exactly your alpha strike; Wean their buffs or go around. Against Necropolis: win by week 4 or watch the snowball bury the swarm; also note their undead are Sleep-immune just like your Spawns — the control spells fizzle in both directions. Against Grove: race their casters — every round you give Faleor and Halon is a stack you lose. Full matchup grid: STUB.

docs.title: Common mistakes
docs.text:
• Playing Hive patiently. The faction's mid/late curve (3/5) exists precisely because its early tempo (4/5) is the win condition — hesitation converts your best phase into your worst.
  • Spending Fire Larvae like real units instead of screens — they're the faction's renewable resource; their job is dying usefully.
  • Ignoring Focus economy on an ability-keyword faction.
  • Underestimating enemy Silence in caster matchups.

docs.title: Verdict: ★★★☆☆ (3/5)
docs.text:
The highest skill ceiling in the game attached to a genuinely scary tempo plan — and the honest rating reflects that most players won't pilot it to its potential. In expert hands with Mila's Masterful Haste or a completed Progenitor arc, it plays a full tier above this score; on autopilot, a full tier below.

Sourcing note: faction identity from the official Factions page; Herald class and subclasses from the official Herald page; roster tree from the official Units navigation; Mila/Leira from official hero pages; Hive Queen adjustment from official patch notes (retrieved 2026-07-03). Summon Swarm full text and unit stat tables are open gaps logged in the factions audit. Builds, ratings, and verdicts are original analysis.

docs.title: Necropolis — Shadowspire's Endless Dead
docs.text:
⭐ Power: 4/5 · 🟢 Beginner Friendly: 4/5 · ⚔️ Campaign: 5/5 · 💰 Economy: 4/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 3/5 · 📈 Early 3/5 · Mid 4/5 · Late 5/5

In one sentence: The attrition faction — every battle you win makes your army bigger through Necromancy, your units drain strength from enemies as they fight, and given enough time you simply cannot be out-grown.

Identity (per the official faction page): ruled by the Necromancers' Guild of Shadowspire; undead; keywords Strong Debuffs & Curses and Improving army by raising the Dead. Community consensus rates it the best late-game faction and, alongside Dungeon, one of the two easiest to win with — the snowball takes care of itself.

docs.title: Faction skill: Necromancy
docs.text:
Known by all Necropolis hero classes (Death Knights and their magic counterparts), unavailable elsewhere.

What it does (official mechanics, verified): After winning a battle, transforms 20% of the strongest enemy stack into friendly Undead of the same tier. This consumes Necromantic Energy, which stores up to 1,500 and refills each week (or when the hero has no stack of that tier). Crucially, Necromancy works on all creatures, not just the Undead — every living enemy is future army. Documented subskills include: native-terrain bonus (+10% dealt / -10% taken on Deathland), +4 mana per battle victory, and a granted ability that heals a friendly Undead stack and revives its fallen units. The Necromancer class additionally spawns a temporary friendly Wight stack whenever an enemy stack dies in battle.

The companion tool — Necromancer Will (Neutral spell, 0 mana): toggles raising off, banking Necromantic Energy instead. This is the skill's hidden depth: raising junk-tier troops dilutes a refined army, so experienced players toggle off against low-value enemies and bank the energy for better conversions. See the Neutral Magic chapter's entry — it's rated 5/5 for this faction specifically.

Why it defines the faction: every other faction pays gold to grow; Necropolis grows by fighting. The correct Necropolis campaign is a deliberately high-volume one — take every fight the map offers, because each one is a recruitment event.

docs.title: Unit roster (full stats, verified against the official wiki)
docs.text: Native terrain: Deathland. All units are Undead creature-type.

docs.title: Tier 1: Skeleton → Skeleton Warrior / Skeleton Archer
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Growth Notes Skeleton 35g 6 4/1 1–3 4 3 30/wk Melee Skeleton Warrior 50g 6 6/3 2–3 6 3 30 Melee Skeleton Archer 50g 6 4/1 1–3 4 3 30 Ranged

The cheapest units in the game (35g!) at the game's highest growth rate (30/week) — before Necromancy adds more. Skeleton Archer is the faction-defining pick: a ranged stack that grows every battle is the engine everything else protects.

docs.title: Tier 2: Wight → Wraith / Phantasm
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Notes Wight 105g 9 2/0 2–4 6 3 Flying; Memory Devourer I (20% dmg dealt → HP, can create temp units) Wraith 140g 9 6/0 2–4 7 4 MD II (40%); Howl of the Banshee (forces enemy to random adjacent hex) Phantasm 140g 9 6/4 2–4 7 4 MD I + Nightmares (stacking 1-pure-dmg curse on hit)

The sustain identity starts here: Wights convert damage into extra temporary units. Wraith for the displacement utility (shoving enemies off chokepoints); Phantasm for durability + curse chip.

docs.title: Tier 3: Undead Pet → Barghest / Armored Hound
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Notes Undead Pet 120g 14 4/6 3–5 5 5 Melee Defense I (-30% melee taken) Barghest 160g 14 8/6 3–5 8 6 + Bone Crusher (steals 1 Def per strike, undispellable) + Call of the Pack (summons 20% of stack) Armored Hound 160g 18 4/8 3–5 5 5 + Protect the Fallen (+3 Def per friendly death) + Lacerated Wounds (stacking bleed)

Correction from the roster-tree draft: Undead Pet is the T3 base, upgrading into Barghest or Armored Hound. The community hype around this line is earned — -30% melee taken on the whole line makes it the best cheap screen in the game. Barghest for tempo (Init 8, Spd 6, self-summoning); Armored Hound for the anvil.

docs.title: Tier 4: Graverobber → Merchant of Death / Kennelmaster
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Notes Graverobber 260g 30 13/9 7–9 2 3 Long Reach; Dig Up Bones (summons 2 Skeletons per unit!) Merchant of Death 360g 30 13/9 7–9 5 4 + stacking poison on attacks + Ensnare Memories (summons Wights) Kennelmaster 360g 30 13/13 8–10 4 4 + Fetch (summons Undead Pets) + Hail! (marks enemy: always takes max damage)

The glue line: every version manufactures more army mid-fight. A 20-Graverobber stack conjures 40 free Skeletons per activation. Kennelmaster's Hail! is Masterful-Vulnerability-as-a-creature-ability — extraordinary on a T4.

docs.title: Tier 5: Lich → Pestilent Lich / Sanguine Lich
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Notes Lich 320g 50 12/12 11–16 4 2 Ranged; Rewind Death (heals 20 HP/lich, revives dead) Pestilent Lich 450g 50 17/12 16 fixed 5 3 Area Strike (splash on every attack) + Death's Embrace (detonates all effects on target at full remaining value) Sanguine Lich 450g 50 12/16 11–16 4 3 Unlife, Undeath (20% of dmg dealt heals whole army) + 10% bonus XP + Rewind Death at 30 HP/lich

The ranged carry. Pestilent is the damage pick (AoE volleys + a devastating combo with your own stacking curses via Death's Embrace); Sanguine turns the stack into an army-wide sustain engine. Both carry creature-level resurrection — Rewind Life as an ability, no spellbook required.

docs.title: Tier 6: Dread Knight → Avatar of War / Hollow Reaper
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Notes Dread Knight 800g 100 19/18 12–14 9 6 Double Strike; -1 enemy morale aura Avatar of War 1300g 100 19/20 18–20 11 7 Double Strike; -2 morale aura; steals 1 Atk per strike (undispellable) Hollow Reaper 1300g 100 24/18 22–24 10 6 -2 morale aura; +3 Atk per friendly death; Death Stare: kills +1 unit per attack, up to tier 7

The community's "six Dread Knights beat ten Temple elites" reputation checks out in the numbers: Double Strike means Avatar of War effectively swings 36–40 per unit. Hollow Reaper trades Double Strike for a guaranteed bonus kill per attack — the anti-elite pick.

docs.title: Tier 7: Vampire → Vampire Lord / Vampire Scholar
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Notes Vampire 1200g + 1 Mercury 150 25/20 25 fixed 11 5 Flying; Vampirism (40% of dmg dealt → HP, revives fallen) Vampire Lord 2000g + 2 Mercury 175 25/27 30 13 7 + Swift Strike (no enemy counterattacks, ever) Vampire Scholar 2000g + 2 Mercury 150 27/20 30 12 5 Long Reach; Blood Transfusion (drain enemy → heal/revive ally) + Corpse Explosion

The faction thesis as a T7: a flying lifedrinker whose stack refills itself and revives its own dead mid-combat. Vampire Lord is the front-line monster (attacks that are never punished + 40% drain = near-unkillable in sustained melee); Scholar is the support pick, converting enemy HP into ally revival. Note the Mercury cost — this faction's rare-resource bottleneck.

docs.title: Recommended magic schools (cross-referenced)
docs.text:
Primary: Nightshade. The faction text says "Strong Debuffs & Curses" and the school delivers exactly that; better, undead immunity to several Nightshade tools (Sleep can't target your own troops in mirror situations) means you suffer the school's downsides less than anyone. Key entries: Despair (stackable DoT — undead armies grind long fights where it shines), Vulnerability, and Web — specialist hero Marl (Death Knight) applies his from the start of battle, the perfect screen for a skeleton-archer core.

Primary co-pick: Arcane, for one spell — Rewind Life. Lord Rufus's Masterful version revives units beyond their original amount, which on top of Necromancy makes Necropolis the only faction with two army-growth engines running simultaneously. A Rufus campaign genuinely ends fights with more units than it started, from two directions at once. (And Mag — the Arcane school-master, per the master hero table — casts every Arcane spell a level higher while locking the enemy out of the school entirely: two elite Arcane mains in one faction.)

Avoid leaning on: Daylight's healing suite — Heavenly Blades' heal explicitly excludes undead, and the school's living-army assumptions run through several entries. Cherry-pick Radiant Armour; skip the rest.

docs.title: Hero picks
docs.text:
• Lord Rufus (the campaign engine): Masterful Rewind Life. The double-growth loop described above; arguably the strongest campaign-economy hero in the game. (Corrected attribution — earlier drafts credited this to Mag.)
  • Mag (the school-master): +1 level to every Arcane spell, no same-school lockout, and the enemy hero cannot cast Arcane at all — the other elite Necromancer main.
  • Marl (the control general): Web specialist from battle start — pairs with the ranged skeleton core to keep melee threats permanently out of reach.
  • Laura (Masterful Despair): the stackable-DoT school's missing owner, now confirmed — her all-enemy-types version plus Sorcery start makes her the curse build's captain.
  • Shadespinner Oona (Nightshade school-master): +1 level to all Nightshade spells and the enemy can't cast the school — the debuff faction's debuff queen.
  • Bulwark: the physical-damage specialist for players who'd rather swing than cast.

docs.title: Three builds
docs.text:
1. The Rising Tide (beginner): mass Skeleton Archers behind a Wight/Barghest screen; fight everything on the map; let Necromancy compound. The build that plays itself.

2. The Drain Engine (mid-game): Vampire-line core + Liches, hero on Nightshade (Despair + Vulnerability). Long fights favor you twice — lifedrain sustains the front while curses grind the enemy down.

3. Rufus's Paradox (late-game): any quality core + Lord Rufus casting Masterful Rewind Life; deliberately trade stacks knowing they return larger. The build that breaks the game's attrition math outright.

docs.title: Matchups (vs. completed chapters)
docs.text: Against Dungeon: the classic tempo-vs-inevitability matchup — Dungeon must win by week 4–5 (per the Dungeon chapter's own curve) because every week after belongs to the snowball. Against Temple: Fatal Decay (Nightshade, 4 mana) is Temple's scariest tool against you — it switches off both lifedrain and post-battle logic in the fights where it's cast; likewise expect Daylight cleanses to strip your curse stack. Full matchup grid: STUB.

docs.title: Common mistakes
docs.text:
• Avoiding fights to "preserve the army" — backwards for this faction; fights are the economy.
  • Leaving Necromancy raising junk on autopilot instead of toggling Necromancer Will (see the Neutral chapter).
  • Learning Sleep or living-heal spells with no legal targets in the mirror or on your own troops.
  • Racing to T7 before the skeleton engine is running — the pyramid is built from the bottom here.

docs.title: Verdict: ★★★★☆ (4/5)
docs.text:
The best pure campaign faction in the game — its growth loop converts time into inevitability, and Mag's Rewind Life turns that loop into a flywheel. Held from 5/5 by a slower early game than Dungeon's and a gameplan that, while dominant, is vulnerable to the small set of tools (Fatal Decay chief among them) designed specifically to switch it off.

Sourcing note: faction identity from the official Factions page; roster tree from the official Units navigation (retrieved 2026-07-03); Necromancy mechanics from the official Skills/Units pages; hero data from official patch notes and the magic chapters' confirmed pairings. Full unit stat tables are an open gap logged in the factions audit. Builds, ratings, and verdicts are original analysis.

docs.title: Schism — The Sundered Depths
docs.text:
⭐ Power: 3/5 · 🟢 Beginner Friendly: 2/5 · ⚔️ Campaign: 3/5 · 💰 Economy: 3/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 4/5 · 📈 Early 3/5 · Mid 4/5 · Late 3/5

In one sentence: The game's strangest faction — abyssal cultists and their otherworldly Ra'Shoth beasts, defined by a faction skill that conjures extra units into every battle before it begins, and by resurrection mechanics that make its armies harder to keep dead than their size suggests.

Identity: the newest-feeling design of the six (internally coded "unfrozen" — a nod to developer Unfrozen — in the wiki's own module data), with an ice-and-abyss theme visible across its heroes (Icequeen Hel'Ghat) and cult-structured roster (Cultists, Binders). The most patch-adjusted faction in recent official notes — the developers explicitly reverted and re-tuned Schism changes after Patch 8 for over-impacting single-hero mode, which tells you both that it's powerful in the right mode and that its balance is still settling.

docs.title: Faction skill: Abyssal Communion
docs.text:
The Schism-exclusive skill. What's officially documented (Skills page): "Schism heroes can summon additional units at the start of the battle." Free bonus units in every fight before a single turn is taken — the tempo equivalent of starting every battle a stack ahead.

The full mechanic (verified this pass): every Schism hero carries a personal Communion level that rises by one per battle victory and halves at the start of each day. At the start of every combat, temporary reinforcement units join the army based on the current Communion level — they exist only for that fight. The loop this creates is the faction: consecutive wins snowball your battle-start bonus; a single day of idleness or a lost fight bleeds it away. Each hero tracks Communion separately, so concentrating fights on one hero is usually right. Like Necromancy, it's an army-from-nowhere skill — but front-loaded into combat and streak-dependent rather than banked.

docs.title: Unit roster (tree complete; stats verified where sourced)
docs.text:
Native terrain: Snow. The full seven-tier tree, completed this pass from the official Units navigation:

Tier Base Upgrade paths T1 Ra'Shoth Stinging Ra'Shoth / Ferocious Ra'Shoth T2 Cultist Binder / Votary T3 Aga'Shoth Rider Aga'Shoth Tamer / Aga'Shoth Mat'ha T4 Grand Shoth Unspeakable Shoth / Unthinkable Shoth T5 Concubus Mistress of Chains / Bewitcher T6 Arbitrator Rift Arbitrator / Bloated Arbitrator T7 Abyssal Overseer line Abyssal Envoy / Abyssal Executor

Verified unit stats (from official/first-party unit pages):

Unit Tier Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Key traits Concubus T5 — 60 16/13 14–20 5 2 "Earn Your Magic, Dear" (enemy hero's spells cost +1 mana); Handcuffs (blocks a target's active abilities); ranged with Nimbleness (shoots even when adjacent) Abyssal Envoy T7 2,500g 250 30/30 40–60 9 5 Absolute Resistance: immune to ALL Magic Damage; Summoning Rite (once per battle, raises an Envoy stack from a friendly corpse at 50% HP — permanent, survives the battle)

Remaining per-unit rows (T1–T4, T6, upgrade variants): the official Schism Units page currently rejects automated retrieval (bot detection) — logged in the audit; the tree above and the rows below are what independent sourcing confirms.

Roster reading, from verified data: - Resurrection is everywhere: Ra'Shoth and Grand Shoth each carry a once-per-battle Summoning Rite that replaces a destroyed stack (Grand Shoth's at 50% HP, permanently — it survives the battle). Community guidance is emphatic: spend the activation on Grand Shoths, not Ra'Shoths — the tier difference in the returned stack is the whole value. - Cultists (T2) are ranged — the early army's damage while Ra'Shoth and Aga'Shoth Riders screen. Binder adds Long Reach plus a stacking pure-damage curse and counterattack-stripping. - Arbitrators (T6) are the ranged carry and the consensus build-order target: reachable by Week 2, and the unit that compensates for the fragile lower tiers. - The T7 line is magic-immune (Absolute Resistance on the Envoy; Pure Resistance on the Overseer, which also carries Will of the Abyss — a free second action per turn) — meaning Schism supports an Armageddon build exactly like Dungeon's Black Dragons. The faction that suffers most from anti-summon tech owns one of the two rosters that trivialize the game's biggest nuke. - The Envoy's own Summoning Rite raising permanent stacks from corpses makes it an army-growth engine at T7 — Necromancy's logic, one fight at a time.

docs.title: Recommended magic schools (cross-referenced)
docs.text:
Primary: Nightshade. Sister Keiri (Riftspeaker) starts with basic Nightshade Magic per her official page, and she's the school's Umbral Grip specialist — the unmodifiable-damage nuke whose Masterful version hits adjacent enemies. A faction that starts every battle with bonus summoned bodies wants exactly what Nightshade sells: control (Web, Silence) to protect the numbers advantage, and Vulnerability to convert it into kills.

Synergy note — the summon economy: Abyssal Communion's free units + Nightshade's Summon Starchild + (for the truly committed) Primal's Summon Primal Remnant stack into the game's most disposable-bodies army — every screening death is a death that cost nothing. The mirrored warning: Anti-Magic (Primal) and Heavenly Blades L4 (Daylight) both destroy summoned units on contact — Schism suffers more from anti-summon tech than any other faction, full stop. Read those two entries as threat briefings.

docs.title: Hero picks
docs.text:
• The Eye Collective (the consensus best): starts with a Ra'Shoth stack and two Grand Shoth stacks — immediate access to the faction's resurrection mechanic from Day 1, per current community tier assessments.
  • Sister Keiri (the signature): Umbral Grip specialist, Riftspeaker, starts with Abyssal Communion + Nightshade — the faction's designed-for magic main. Her lore twins her with Dungeon's Sister Deira (two sisters, two masks, two factions) — a lovely campaign-flavor pairing the game clearly intends.
  • Ra'Davok / Changeling Urgo: confirmed spell specialists (patch notes cohort); exact spells pending the verification pass.
  • Grellekh the Betrayer / Icequeen Hel'Ghat: the physical-damage specialists — Schism's might route, for armies that lean on the Ra'Shoth line's claws over the Riftspeakers' rifts.

docs.title: Three builds
docs.text:
1. The Opening Rift (core): Abyssal Communion summons screening for a Cultist/ranged core; Sister Keiri deleting priority targets with Umbral Grip. The faction as designed.

2. Endless Congregation (attrition): resurrection-ability units + Summon Starchild stacking — an army that has to be killed twice while its summons die for free. Nightmarish in long fights.

3. Winter's Betrayal (might): Icequeen Hel'Ghat or Grellekh leading a Ra'Shoth-forward physical army, magic reduced to support. The contrarian build for players who want the aesthetic without the caster micromanagement.

docs.title: Matchups (vs. completed chapters)
docs.text: Against Dungeon: their tempo meets your head start — Abyssal Communion's free units blunt exactly the early volleys Dungeon relies on. Against Temple: Heavenly Blades L4 is their anti-summon button; a Temple player who reads their own chapter will bring it. Against Necropolis: the two resurrection factions grinding each other — Fatal Decay (4 mana, Nightshade) swings this mirror more than any army decision, in both directions. Against Hive: both sides' control spells fizzle on the other's immune types; it comes down to whose bodies are cheaper — and yours are free. Full matchup grid: STUB.

docs.title: Common mistakes
docs.text:
• Treating the Communion summons as army — they're ammunition. Spend them screening, absorbing counterattacks, and bodying hexes.
  • Walking into Anti-Magic without a plan B; the faction's worst matchup isn't a faction, it's a spell.
  • Skipping Fatal Decay tech in sustain mirrors.
  • Judging the faction by its current patch state — it's the most actively re-balanced roster in the game; verify against the latest notes before tournaments.

docs.title: Verdict: ★★★☆☆ (3/5)
docs.text:
A genuinely novel design — battle-start summons plus in-combat resurrection is a real identity, not a reskin — that currently sits a skill-investment away from its power. High-knowledge players who route around anti-summon tech get a faction that starts every fight ahead; everyone else gets a roster whose best tricks their opponents can switch off. The most volatile rating in this book, and the one most likely to change with the next balance pass.

Sourcing note: Abyssal Communion's core effect from the official Skills page; partial roster tree from the official Units navigation; hero data from official pages and patch notes; resurrection mechanics as documented in this book's Nightshade chapter sourcing (retrieved 2026-07-03). Full roster, stat tables, and Communion subskills are open gaps logged in the factions audit. Builds, ratings, and verdicts are original analysis.

docs.title: Temple — The Church of the Sun
docs.text:
⭐ Power: 4/5 · 🟢 Beginner Friendly: 5/5 · ⚔️ Campaign: 4/5 · 💰 Economy: 4/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 2/5 · 📈 Early 4/5 · Mid 4/5 · Late 4/5

In one sentence: The classic human faction — well-rounded troops that get extra value from buffs, a comeback-mechanic faction skill, and the game's deepest bench of Daylight spell specialists, making it the most forgiving faction in the game without being weak.

Identity (per the official faction page): governed by the High Inquisition; humans; keywords Unit Empowerment and Strong Unit Protection. Its troops are individually unspectacular and collectively reliable — the faction's power lives in multiplying solid stats with buffs, morale, and hero support. Native terrain: creatures fight better on it (+1 Initiative; +10%/-10% damage with the Righteousness terrain subskill).

docs.title: Faction skill: Righteousness
docs.text:
Known by all Knights and Clerics, unavailable to everyone else.

What it does: Whenever a friendly creature stack dies or kills an enemy stack, the hero's Attack, Defense, Spellpower, and Knowledge all increase by +1 for two rounds (three at higher skill rank). Stackable.

Why it's sneaky-strong: it pays out on both good and bad events — your kills stack it, and your losses stack it too. In a bloody even fight, a Temple hero might sit on +5 to all four stats by round three: their spells hit ~25% harder (Spellpower), their army's damage climbs (Attack), everything takes less (Defense). It's a comeback-and-snowball mechanic in one, and it rewards exactly the big-messy-battle gameplan Temple armies want anyway.

Subskill of note: the native-terrain rank (+10% dealt / -10% taken on Temple ground) effectively makes home-turf defensive battles a 20-point damage swing before anything else happens.

docs.title: Unit roster (full stats, verified against the official wiki)
docs.text: Upgrade format: Base → Upgrade A / Upgrade B (choose a path per the usual dual-upgrade system).

docs.title: Tier 1: Swordsman → Guard Captain / Sun's Aegis
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Swordsman 85g 12 4/4 2–3 5 4 Guard Captain 110g 12 6/5 2–3 6 4 Sun's Aegis 110g 15 5/8 2–3 5 5

The fork is offense (Guard Captain: +2 Atk, +1 Init) vs. wall (Sun's Aegis: +3 HP, +4 Def, +1 Spd). Take Sun's Aegis in most campaigns — Temple's T1 job is absorbing hits while the shooters work, and 15 HP / 8 Def is outstanding tanking per gold at this tier.

docs.title: Tier 2: Crossbowman → Austringer / Marksman
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Notes Crossbowman 105g 10 5/3 3–4 5 Ranged Austringer 140g 10 5/4 3–4 5 Ranged Marksman 140g 10 6/3 3–4 7 Ranged, no range penalty

Marksman is the pick and it isn't close: +2 Initiative and the removal of the distance damage falloff turns a filler shooter into a genuine core stack. No-range-penalty shooters can sit in the safest corner of the map and lose nothing.

docs.title: Tier 3: Griffin → Temple Griffin / Guardian Griffin
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Griffin 255g 25 7/6 5–9 9 5 Temple Griffin 350g 25 8/6 5–9 12 7 Guardian Griffin 350g 25 9/10 7–7 10 6

The faction's tempo unit. Temple Griffin at Initiative 12 acts before almost everything in the early-to-mid game — it's your opener, your shooter-hunter, and your objective-grabber. Guardian Griffin (fixed 7–7 damage, 10 Def) is the steadier attrition pick. Temple Griffin for aggression, Guardian for grind — and note the fixed damage on Guardian pairs beautifully with Masterful Blessing's max-damage philosophy without needing it.

docs.title: Tier 4: Lightweaver → Hierophant / Sun Herald
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Notes Lightweaver 310g 35 11/11 11–11 4 Ranged, Morale 2 Hierophant 440g 35 13/16 11–11 6 Ranged Sun Herald 440g 35 16/13 11–18 5 Ranged

The faction's backbone — and the unit that makes hero Zenith S-tier (she starts with Lightweavers in her opening army, weeks before anyone else can build them). Fixed 11–11 damage means perfectly predictable volleys; the built-in Morale 2 means bonus turns. Sun Herald (11–18, higher Attack) for damage ceilings; Hierophant (16 Def) if your Lightweavers keep getting dived.

docs.title: Tier 5: Cavalry → Noble Cavalry / Sunspear Cavalry
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Cavalry 850g 85 12/17 10–14 10 7 Noble Cavalry 1220g 85 12/17 12–16 13 7 Sunspear Cavalry 1220g 85 14/22 12–16 10 8, Morale 2

The hammer to the infantry's anvil. Noble Cavalry at Initiative 13 is the fastest-acting thing Temple fields until Archangels — first-strike charges that delete a shooter stack before it fires. Sunspear trades that tempo for 22 Defense and morale. Noble for alpha strikes, Sunspear for lines that hold.

docs.title: Tier 6: Inquisitor → Mother Superior / Excommunicator
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Inquisitor 1000g 90 20/24 19–23 7 4 Mother Superior 1500g 125 20/28 19–23 9 5 Excommunicator 1500g 90 26/24 23–33 8 6

The starkest upgrade fork in the roster: Mother Superior is a fortress (125 HP, 28 Def), Excommunicator is a blender (23–33 damage, 26 Atk). The names telegraph the play: Mother Superior anchors defensive compositions; Excommunicator ends fights.

docs.title: Tier 7: Angel → Archangel / Apotheosis
docs.text:
Unit Cost HP Atk/Def Dmg Init Spd Notes Angel 2400g + 1 Gem 225 30/30 50–75 8 4 Ranged Archangel 4000g + 2 Gem 225 35/30 50–75 12 5 Ranged, no penalties Apotheosis 4000g + 2 Gem 300 30/35 50–75 10 10 Ranged

A ranged tier 7 — one of Temple's defining luxuries. Angels deal their 50–75 without ever taking a counterattack. Archangel (Initiative 12, no range/melee penalties of any kind) is the flexible pick; Apotheosis (300 HP, Speed 10) is a flying fortress that repositions across the entire board in one move. Note the Gem cost mirrors Dungeon's dragon economy — budget Gems from mid-game.

docs.title: Recommended magic schools (cross-referenced to the magic chapters)
docs.text:
Primary: Daylight — this is not a suggestion, it's the faction's second identity. Temple's "troops gain extra benefits from buffs" faction text is a direct pointer, and the hero roster seals it: four of the game's confirmed Masterful-Daylight/adjacent specialists are Temple Clerics — Vesper (Masterful Blessing: always max damage), Julius (Masterful Healing Water: heals + cleanses), Anastasia the Meek (Masterful Arina's Touch: +Speed), and the crossover pick Nadir (Masterful Vulnerability, a Nightshade spell — always-max-damage against a marked target). Key entries to read in the Daylight chapter: Blessing and Favorable Wind (your Marksmen/Lightweavers love them), Radiant Armour (the L4 army-wide -35% turns Temple's durable line unkillable), and Judgement for the late-game nuke Righteousness's stacking Spellpower quietly enables.

Secondary: Arcane for Rewind Life — Temple's whole gameplan is preserving a quality army, and the resurrection spell is the strongest preservation tool in the game.

Situational tech: Nadir makes a genuine hybrid possible — Daylight buffs on your army, Masterful Vulnerability on their key stack, then concentrated fire. It's the single strongest cross-school package any faction gets natively.

docs.title: Hero picks
docs.text:
• Zenith (S-tier pick): starts with Lightweavers — a T4 core weeks early — plus a specialization that boosts their production and stats. The tempo advantage compounds all game.
