In battle: Wall off the two open lanes to your 20 Dancers and an enemy melee stack must either detour (a lost turn — ~120 of its damage delayed) or spend an attack breaking crystal (its entire ~120 swing hitting rock instead of your units). Either way, one 8-mana cast bought your shooters a free volley. This is also the setup half of the Primal trap game: walls dictate the path, Firewall and mines punish it. The skill ceiling is pure geometry — a badly-placed wall protects nothing.

All four levels: - L1: Place up to 2 crystal obstacles on free hexes; each dies to 1 hit. Duration: 2 rounds. - L2: Duration rises to 3 rounds. - L3: Obstacles rise to 3. - L4: Each obstacle takes 2 hits to destroy.

Masterful version: None documented.

Why you'd cast it: Terrain is the one free resource on every map — this spell prints more of it.

Best heroes: Ranged-core defenders and chokepoint architects.

When it's weak: Flyers ignore walls entirely.

Common mistakes: Walling a lane the enemy wasn't using anyway.

Campaign Verdict: ★★★☆☆ (3/5) — quiet, positional, better in every fight that has a geometry.

docs.title: Ice Bolt
docs.text:
Mana cost: 10

⭐ 4/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 2/5 · 📈 Early 4/5 · Mid 4/5 · Late 3/5

Plain English: A frost bolt that hurts and chills — damage plus an Initiative penalty pushing the target later in the turn order.

The Math: Damage = base + rate × Spellpower; the chill is a flat Initiative cut lasting 2 rounds.

Level Formula / effect Result at SP 10 L1 75 + 10×SP, -2 Initiative 175 dmg L2 150 + 10×SP 250 dmg L3 150 + 15×SP 300 dmg L4 Initiative -2 → -4 300 dmg + deep chill

In battle: Compare directly against its sibling: at equal levels Ice Bolt out-damages Lightning Bolt (175 vs 125 at L1, same Spellpower) for 4 more mana — and staples a control effect on top. The -4 Initiative at L4 is the real payload against elite units: an enemy tier-6 at Initiative 7 drops to 3, now acting after your Initiative-4 Dancers — 300 damage plus a stolen turn-order slot in one cast. Aim it at the enemy's fastest threat, always: the damage is generic, the chill is targeted.

All four levels: - L1: Deals 75 + (10 × Spellpower) Magic Damage and -2 Initiative for 2 rounds. - L2: Base damage rises from 75 to 150. - L3: Per-Spellpower rate rises from 10 to 15. - L4: Initiative penalty deepens from -2 to -4.

Masterful version: The Initiative penalty is twice as strong (-8 at spell L4). Hero: not yet identified in available sourcing.

Why you'd cast it: Damage you were going to buy anyway, with tempo theft included.

Best heroes: Any Primal hero past Week 2; supersedes Lightning Bolt as the default bolt.

When it's weak: Against slow armies already acting last, the chill half is wasted.

Common mistakes: Aiming at whatever's wounded instead of whatever's fast.

Campaign Verdict: ★★★★☆ (4/5) — the thinking player's Lightning Bolt.

docs.title: Stone Fangs
docs.text:
Mana cost: 18

⭐ 3/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 2/5 · 📈 Early 2/5 · Mid 4/5 · Late 3/5

Plain English: Stone spikes erupt under several different enemy stacks at once — multi-target damage without needing them clustered.

The Math: Damage = base + rate × Spellpower, to each of up to 3–4 separate targets:

Level Formula / targets Per-target at SP 10 Total (max targets) L1 50 + 5×SP, 3 targets 100 300 L2 100 + 5×SP 150 450 L3 targets 3 → 4 150 600 L4 100 + 10×SP 200 800

In battle: This is Fireball for enemies who refuse to clump: L3 hits four stacks anywhere on the board for 150 each — 8 Dancer-class units dead per stack, no positioning required from them or you. Its total output at L4 (800) actually beats same-level Fireball against spread formations, at higher mana. The two spells are a matched pair: scout the enemy's formation, then bring the one their spacing is wrong for.

All four levels: - L1: Deals 50 + (5 × Spellpower) Magic Damage to up to 3 different enemy targets. - L2: Base damage rises from 50 to 100. - L3: Targets rise from 3 to 4. - L4: Per-Spellpower rate rises from 5 to 10.

Masterful version: None documented.

Why you'd cast it: Cluster-proof area damage — the answer when the enemy spreads to dodge your Fireballs.

Best heroes: Primal specialists rounding out the AoE toolkit.

When it's weak: Against 1–2 stack armies, most of its targets go unused.

Common mistakes: Casting into a clustered enemy where Fireball would've been cheaper for the same result.

Campaign Verdict: ★★★☆☆ (3/5) — the formation-agnostic half of Primal's AoE pair.

docs.title: Firewall
docs.text:
Mana cost: 12

⭐ 3/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 4/5 · 📈 Early 2/5 · Mid 3/5 · Late 3/5

Plain English: Sets hexes on fire — any ground unit stepping onto a burning hex takes damage. A damage-dealing fence.

The Math: Damage per trigger = 60 + 10 × Spellpower → 160 at Spellpower 10, per creature, per burning hex entered. Duration: 2 rounds.

Level Effect Value at SP 10 L1 2 burning hexes 160/trigger L2 Mana 12 → 9 160 L3 Hexes 2 → 3 160 L4 +50% damage 240/trigger

In battle: Lay L4 fire across the only approach lane to your Dancers and every enemy stack that pushes through pays 240 — 13 Dancer-class units per stack, per crossing — before ever swinging. Two stacks forced through is 480 damage from one 9-mana cast (L2 discount). Like all Primal terrain, it's a bet on movement: paired with Crystal Crown walls that leave exactly one lane open, it stops being a bet and becomes a toll booth.

All four levels: - L1: Burns 2 selected hexes; a ground creature stepping on one takes 60 + (10 × Spellpower) Magic Damage. Duration: 2 rounds. - L2: Mana cost drops from 12 to 9. - L3: Burning hexes rise from 2 to 3. - L4: Damage rises by +50%.

Masterful version: This version stays active +1 round. Hero: not yet identified in available sourcing.

Why you'd cast it: Damage the enemy walks into voluntarily is damage your army never has to earn.

Best heroes: The Crystal Crown/trap-game architects; defensive siege players.

When it's weak: Flyers, again — Primal's whole terrain suite shares this blind spot.

Common mistakes: Burning hexes beside the lane instead of in it — fire nobody touches is 9 wasted mana.

Campaign Verdict: ★★★☆☆ (3/5) — a toll booth in spell form; worth a star more inside a dedicated terrain build.

docs.title: Cave In
docs.text:
Mana cost: 10

⭐ 3/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 3/5 · 📈 Early 2/5 · Mid 3/5 · Late 3/5

Plain English: Drops the ceiling on an enemy — direct damage, plus the rubble fills every empty hex around them, walling the survivor in.

The Math: Damage = base + rate × Spellpower; obstacles last 1 round (2 at L3), dying to 1 hit each:

Level Formula / effect Result at SP 10 L1 50 + 15×SP, rubble walls 200 dmg L2 100 + 15×SP 250 L3 Rubble lasts +1 round 250, longer wall L4 100 + 20×SP 300

In battle: Two spells in one cast: 250 damage (14 Dancer-class units at L2) plus an instant prison — a melee stack buried in rubble must spend its next action breaking out instead of attacking, which is a second ~120-damage turn deleted on top of the direct hit. Best target: a dangerous melee stack mid-advance, ideally one your Web has already slowed, turning "delayed" into "entombed."

All four levels: - L1: Deals 50 + (15 × Spellpower) Magic Damage to the target; all adjacent empty hexes fill with rock obstacles (1 hit each, 1 round). - L2: Base damage rises from 50 to 100. - L3: Obstacles last +1 round. - L4: Per-Spellpower rate rises from 15 to 20.

Masterful version: The rubble obstacles require +1 more hit to destroy. Hero: not yet identified in available sourcing.

Why you'd cast it: Damage plus a stolen enemy turn, priced like plain damage.

Best heroes: Control-leaning Primal casters.

When it's weak: Against targets standing beside their allies — occupied hexes can't fill with rubble, so the prison has doors.

Common mistakes: Casting at a stack in the middle of the enemy formation, where almost no hexes are empty to wall.

Campaign Verdict: ★★★☆☆ (3/5) — the isolated-target punisher; target selection is the whole spell.

docs.title: Earth's Rage
docs.text:
Mana cost: 16

⭐ 3/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 2/5 · 📈 Early 1/5 · Mid 4/5 (sieges) · Late 4/5 (sieges)

Plain English: The siege-breaker — an earthquake that batters every castle wall, tower, and gate at once, and shakes the defenders huddled behind them.

The Math: Structure damage = 100 + 15 × Spellpower; defender damage = 15 × Spellpower:

Level Structures (SP 10) Creatures inside walls (SP 10) L1 250 to all structures 150 L2 350 150 L3 350 150 + 100 flat L4 550 250

In battle: Sieges are where attacking armies go to die — walls let a smaller garrison shred a bigger army at range while it queues at the gate. One L2 cast dealing 350 to every defensive structure simultaneously collapses that math; two casts level most castles entirely, and the splash (150–250 to every defender inside) means the garrison bleeds while its cover crumbles. Outside sieges the spell does literally nothing, which is exactly the trade: a dead slot on the open field, the single most important spell in the book at a castle gate.

All four levels: - L1: Deals 100 + (15 × Spellpower) damage to all defensive structures; also deals (15 × Spellpower) to all creatures within castle walls. - L2: Structure damage base rises from 100 to 200. - L3: Also deals a flat 100 to all creatures within walls. - L4: Structure base rises from 200 to 400.

Masterful version: None documented.

Why you'd cast it: Every campaign funnels toward castle assaults; this converts them from meatgrinders into formalities.

Best heroes: Whoever leads your castle-taking army.

When it's weak: 100% of non-siege battles.

Common mistakes: Skipping it because most fights aren't sieges — the fights that are decide the campaign.

Campaign Verdict: ★★★☆☆ (3/5) — a specialist scored on average; score it 5/5 the day you're outside enemy walls.

docs.title: Anti-Magic
docs.text:
Mana cost: 18

⭐ 4/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 4/5 · 📈 Early 1/5 · Mid 3/5 · Late 4/5

Plain English: Makes any target — yours or theirs — immune to spells below a tier threshold. Undispellable. Also instantly destroys any summoned or temporary units in the target stack.

The Math: No damage numbers; the scaling is coverage:

Level Immunity blocks Duration L1 Tier 1–3 spells 1 round L2 Tier 1–3 2 rounds L3 Tier 1–4 2 rounds L4 Tier 1–5 — total spell immunity 2 rounds

In battle: Dual-use by design. On your own win-condition stack, L4 means the enemy caster's entire spellbook — their Berserk, their Vulnerability, their Judgement — bounces off for 2 rounds while your stack works. On an enemy stack, it locks their own hero out of buffing or healing it. And the summon-destruction rider is a free third mode: cast it at a Shadow Army-swollen or Starchild stack and the temporary units die instantly — 18+ summoned bodies deleted before the immunity even matters. Three spells wearing one 18-mana coat.

All four levels: - L1: Chosen target becomes immune to tier 1–3 spells; undispellable. Summoned/temporary units in the stack are destroyed. - L2: Base duration rises from 1 to 2 rounds. - L3: Immunity extends to tier 4 spells. - L4: Immunity extends to tier 5 — full spell immunity.

Masterful version: None documented.

Why you'd cast it: The definitive answer to enemy casters — and the hard counter to every summon build in this book.

Best heroes: Anyone facing magic-hero opponents; mandatory tech in caster-vs-caster metas.

When it's weak: Against Might heroes with empty spellbooks, only the anti-summon rider has value.

Common mistakes: Forgetting immunity blocks your spells on that target too — no healing the stack you just sealed.

Campaign Verdict: ★★★★☆ (4/5) — the game's cleanest silence-the- wizard button.

docs.title: Chain Lightning
docs.text:
Mana cost: 24

⭐ 5/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 3/5 · 📈 Early 1/5 · Mid 4/5 · Late 5/5

Plain English: A lightning strike that leaps from the target to nearby enemies, again and again — each jump hitting for half of the last.

The Math: First hit = base + rate × Spellpower; each jump deals -50% of the previous:

Level First hit (SP 10) Jumps Chain total (SP 10) L1 100+20×SP = 300 1 ~450 L2 300 2 ~525 L3 300+20×SP = 500 3 ~940 L4 300+30×SP = 600 4 ~1,160

In battle: At L3, the chain opens with 500 (annihilating a 360-HP Dancer-class stack outright) then leaps for 250, 125, 62 — nearly a thousand total damage across four stacks from one 24-mana cast. The jump targeting is random within 3 hexes, which is the one wrinkle: against mixed positions it can arc into weird targets, so it's at its cleanest when everything nearby is an enemy. The famous caveat from older Heroes games doesn't apply here as written — the wiki text says jumps go to targets within range, so mind your own units' spacing all the same.

All four levels: - L1: Deals 100 + (20 × Spellpower) Magic Damage; jumps 1 time to a random target within 3 hexes at -50% per jump. - L2: +1 jump. - L3: +1 jump; base damage rises 100 → 300. - L4: +1 jump; per-Spellpower rate rises 20 → 30.

Masterful version: Loses half as much damage per jump (-25% instead of -50%) — at L4 that pushes the chain total toward ~1,800. Hero: Halon (Grove) starts with Masterful Chain Lightning as his specialization — confirmed against hero sourcing.

Why you'd cast it: The highest total damage of any spell in the game when the jumps land — Primal's crown.

Best heroes: Halon, spectacularly; any late-game Primal specialist otherwise.

When it's weak: Sparse battlefields with nothing in jump range — the chain fizzles to a pricey single bolt.

Common mistakes: Casting into a melee scrum where a jump can clip your own engaged stack.

Campaign Verdict: ★★★★★ (5/5) — the signature spell of the school and the game's best sustained-value nuke.

docs.title: Circle of Winter
docs.text:
Mana cost: 19

⭐ 4/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 3/5 · 📈 Early 1/5 · Mid 3/5 · Late 4/5

Plain English: A ring of frost that blasts everything around a point — and physically shoves the survivors away from it. Black Hole's opposite twin.

The Math: Damage = 75 + rate × Spellpower to everything adjacent to the target hex:

Level Formula / effect Result at SP 10 L1 75 + 15×SP, push away 225 L2 75 + 20×SP 275 L3 + all targets -2 Initiative 275 + chill L4 Doubles base damage and Initiative cut 425, -4 Initiative

In battle: Drop it in the middle of an enemy advance and every adjacent stack takes 275 (L2 — 15 Dancer-class units each) and gets knocked backward — melee attackers who'd reached your line are suddenly a hex short again, buying your shooters another full volley (~120) they weren't owed. Where Black Hole gathers enemies for your follow-up AoE, Circle of Winter scatters them away from your army: the choice between the twins is the choice between "set up my next nuke" and "get these things off me."

All four levels: - L1: Deals 75 + (15 × Spellpower) Magic Damage to all creatures adjacent to the target hex; pushes affected stacks away from center. - L2: Per-Spellpower rate rises from 15 to 20. - L3: All targets also lose 2 Initiative. - L4: Base damage and Initiative reduction both double.

Masterful version: None documented.

Why you'd cast it: Damage plus a panic button — the "off my lawn" spell for shooter armies under melee pressure.

Best heroes: Ranged-core generals; the perfect emergency cast the turn the enemy makes contact.

When it's weak: When you're the melee aggressor, pushing the enemy away helps them.

Common mistakes: Using it as pure damage when the push is pointed the wrong way for your plan.

Campaign Verdict: ★★★★☆ (4/5) — Primal's best defensive tool, disguised as another nuke.

docs.title: Primordial Chaos
docs.text:
Mana cost: 17

⭐ 2/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 4/5 · 📈 Early 2/5 · Mid 3/5 · Late 2/5

Plain English: A map spell — rerolls a neutral army into a different army of the same overall strength (and at higher levels, a slightly weaker one).

The Math: Range = multiplier × Sight; once per day:

Level Effect L1 Transform neutral squad within 1×Sight into equal-strength squad L2 Radius 1× → 2× Sight L3 New squad is 5% weaker L4 Weakening 5% → 10%

All four levels: - L1: Transforms a neutral squad within 1 × Sight into a different squad of the same strength. Once per day. - L2: Radius multiplier rises to 2×. - L3: The transformed squad's strength is reduced by 5%. - L4: Reduction deepens to 10%.

Masterful version: None documented.

Why you'd cast it: Rerolling a guard your army can't fight (ranged nightmare, hard-counter composition) into one it can — the match-fixing spell. The 10% shave at L4 is a bonus, not the point.

Best heroes: Experienced players who know which neutral types their army beats cheaply and which it doesn't.

When it's weak: The reroll is random — you can trade a bad matchup for a worse one.

Common mistakes: Rerolling fights you'd already win cleanly, gambling value you already had.

Campaign Verdict: ★★☆☆☆ (2/5) — a connoisseur's tool; clever, but the variance keeps the rating honest.

docs.title: Armageddon
docs.text:
Mana cost: 40

⭐ 4/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 5/5 · 📈 Early 1/5 · Mid 1/5 · Late 5/5

Plain English: The end of the world, locally — damages every creature on the battlefield. Theirs, and yours.

The Math: Damage = base + rate × Spellpower, to everything:

Level Formula Result at SP 10 (per stack, both sides) L1 100 + 10×SP 200 L2 200 + 10×SP 300 L3 300 + 10×SP 400 L4 300 + 20×SP 500

In battle: The raw total is obscene — against a six-stack enemy army, L4 deals 3,000 damage in one cast. The catch is the other 3,000 it deals to you, which is why the spell is really a build, not a button: it belongs to armies that are immune to it. Dungeon's Black Dragons (spell-immune) are the canonical partner — an army of Dragons plus L4 Armageddon deletes 500 HP from every enemy stack per cast while your own side doesn't notice the sky falling. Without an immune or heavily-resistant army, casting it is an elaborate way to lose.

All four levels: - L1: Deals 100 + (10 × Spellpower) Magic Damage to all creatures on the battlefield. - L2: Base damage rises from 100 to 200. - L3: Base rises from 200 to 300. - L4: Per-Spellpower rate rises from 10 to 20.

Masterful version: None documented.

Why you'd cast it: With the right army, it's a win condition that no defense answers.

Best heroes: Dungeon Warlocks fielding Black Dragons — the Armageddon-dragon build is a series tradition alive and well here.

When it's weak: With any normal army, catastrophically — self-inflicted 500s are not a strategy.

Common mistakes: Casting it because the number is big. The number is big in both directions.

Campaign Verdict: ★★★★☆ (4/5) — a 5/5 inside its build, unplayable outside it; the average is the rating.

docs.title: Hksmilla's Rampage
docs.text:
Mana cost: 28

⭐ 4/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 3/5 · 📈 Early 1/5 · Mid 2/5 · Late 4/5

Plain English: Sends one of your stacks berserk in the good way — it attacks twice per action, hitting harder, but also taking more damage in its fury.

The Math: Double Strike plus a symmetric damage modifier — dealt and taken. Duration = 1 + 1 per 8 Spellpower (~2 rounds):

Level Damage dealt AND taken L1 ±10% L2 ±15% L3 ±25% L4 ±40%

In battle: On the 20-Dancer stack at L4: each action now fires two volleys at ~168 each (120 × 1.4) — ~336 damage per action, nearly triple baseline — for 2+ rounds. The bill: every hit the Dancers take is also +40%, so an enemy 120 lands as 168, killing 9 instead of 6. The math says the same thing the flavor does: this spell belongs on a stack that will kill what it touches before the retaliation matters — alpha-strike tools, not attrition tools. Pair with Shackles (no counterattacks) and the downside clause never even triggers.

All four levels: - L1: Target friendly creature gains Double Strike and deals and takes +10% damage. - L2: Modifier rises to ±15%. - L3: Modifier rises to ±25%. - L4: Modifier rises to ±40%.

Masterful version: None documented.

Why you'd cast it: The single biggest per-stack damage amplification in the game — Double Strike alone doubles output before the percentage even applies.

Best heroes: Late-game generals with one overwhelming stack and a plan for its safety.

When it's weak: On stacks that will be counterattacked or focused — the ±40% cuts deep in reverse.

Common mistakes: Casting on a tanking stack; the taken-damage clause turns your wall into paper.

Campaign Verdict: ★★★★☆ (4/5) — the finisher's finisher; respect the downside and it wins games.

docs.title: Summon Primal Remnant
docs.text:
Mana cost: 20

⭐ 3/5 · 🧠 Skill Ceiling: 3/5 · 📈 Early 1/5 · Mid 2/5 · Late 3/5

Plain English: Conjures a temporary stack of Primal Remnants — elemental soldiers who fight for you and dissolve when the battle ends.

The Math: Units = 6 + (multiplier × Spellpower):

Level Formula / effect Result at SP 10 L1 6 + 2×SP, 1-hex radius 26 Remnants L2 Radius 1 → 2 26, better placement L3 Can use abilities 26, fully armed L4 Multiplier 2 → 3 36 Remnants

In battle: The direct comparison is Nightshade's Summon Starchild (8 + 1–2 × SP): Primal's version starts bigger and scales harder — 26 vs 18 at L1, 36 vs 28 at L4 — for the same 20 mana, at two tiers higher unlock. Same core value proposition too: free bodies that soak attacks meant for your gold-costing units (every Remnant that dies in a Dancer's place saves 160 gold) and add their own damage every round. Same counters as well — Anti-Magic and Heavenly Blades L4 delete the whole stack on contact.

All four levels: - L1: Summons 6 + (2 × Spellpower) Primal Remnants; they vanish at battle's end. - L2: Summon radius rises from 1 to 2 hexes. - L3: The summoned stack can use its active abilities. - L4: Spellpower multiplier rises from 2 to 3.

Masterful version: None documented.

Why you'd cast it: The biggest free stack in the game, every fight, for heroes who went deep on Primal anyway.

Best heroes: High-Spellpower Primal mains grinding long neutral chains.

When it's weak: Anti-summon tech turns 20 mana into nothing; short fights never repay the cast.

Common mistakes: Summoning behind your line where the Remnants tank nothing and reach nothing.

Campaign Verdict: ★★★☆☆ (3/5) — solid summon value at a tier where its rivals (Chain Lightning, Armageddon) are simply louder.

docs.title: Confirmed Masterful spell / hero pairings (this school)
docs.text:
Spell Masterful effect Hero Status Fireball Hits a bigger area Faleor (Grove) ✅ Confirmed Chain Lightning Loses half as much damage per jump Halon (Grove) ✅ Confirmed Ice Bolt Initiative penalty twice as strong unknown ❌ Hero not identified Firewall Active +1 round unknown ❌ Hero not identified Cave In Rubble takes +1 hit to destroy unknown ❌ Hero not identified

(Notable: both confirmed Primal Masterful heroes are Grove — the "nature" faction is quietly the elemental-damage specialist faction at the hero level, the way Temple is for Daylight.)

docs.title: Synergy notes
docs.text:
• Black Hole (Arcane) → Fireball: the definitive cross-school combo — compress, then incinerate the pile.
  • Crystal Crown → Firewall → Temporal Spheres (Arcane): the full toll-road: walls force the lane, fire and mines tax it.
  • Shackles (Arcane) → Hksmilla's Rampage: Double Strike with the retaliation clause switched off.
  • Black Dragons → Armageddon: the build that makes a 40-mana self-harm spell into a win condition.
  • Web (Nightshade) → Cave In: slow the stack, then bury it — the slowed target can't clear the rubble radius before it triggers again.

docs.title: Common beginner mistakes (school-wide)
docs.text:
• Fireballing your own engaged melee. The radius does not care whose units fill it.
  • Clinging to Lightning Bolt past its early-game prime.
  • Casting Armageddon without an immunity plan.
  • Building the terrain suite (Crown/Firewall) against flying armies that stroll over all of it.
  • Aiming Ice Bolt's chill at slow targets — the control half is the point; spend it on speed.

Sourcing note: all mechanics, mana costs, formulas, per-level effects, and Masterful texts taken directly from the official wiki's Primal Spells table (retrieved 2026-07-03). Faleor→Fireball and Halon→Chain Lightning confirmed against hero sourcing (specialization texts match the wiki's Masterful descriptions verbatim); Ice Bolt, Firewall, and Cave In's Masterful owners are open gaps, logged in the audit. All worked math, battle examples (reference stack: 20 Onyx Dancers), ratings, synergies, and verdicts are original analysis.

docs.title: How the system works (verified)
docs.text:
On level-up a hero gains a random stat point and a choice of three options — new skills or upgrades to learned ones. Every skill has three ranks: Basic → Advanced → Expert.

The depth is in the forks: reaching Advanced offers a choice of 3 subskills (pick one); reaching Expert offers a choice of 2 more, stronger subskills (pick one). You are permanently specializing at every rank-up — with one documented exception: the Holy Sigil of Roph (Epic Relic) grants all unlearned subskills of every faction skill while worn.

Subskills carry cross-skill synergies — explicit "doubled if the hero knows X" clauses that reward planned builds over random rolls (examples below).

docs.title: The full skill list (verified)
docs.text:
Might Skills: Offense · Defense · Leadership · Luck · Resistance · Tactics · Battlecraft · Siegecraft · Recruitment · Combat (Might-hero exclusive)

Magic Skills: Battle Magic · Sorcery · Summon Avatar · Wisdom · Daylight Magic · Nightshade Magic · Arcane Magic · Primal Magic · Thaumaturgy (Magic-hero exclusive)

General Skills: Diplomacy · Logistics · Scouting · Insight · Economy

Faction Skills (auto-known by that faction's heroes, unavailable elsewhere): Righteousness (Temple) · Necromancy (Necropolis) · Murmuring (Grove) · Summon Swarm (Hive) · Abyssal Communion (Schism) · Triumvirate's Strength (Dungeon) — each fully documented in its faction chapter; the four magic-school skills are covered by their spell chapters (each school skill gates Tier 3–5 spells at Basic/Advanced/Expert respectively); Summon Avatar is covered in the Neutral Magic chapter.

docs.title: The Offense tree — a complete worked example (verified)
docs.text:
Rank Effect / subskill options Basic Basic attacks +10% damage Advanced +15% damage, then choose one: Archery (Ranged/Long Reach +15%) · Battle March (+1 Focus Point per attack — doubled if hero knows Luck) · Battle Frenzy (+2 Attack per point of Morale) Expert +20% damage, then choose one: Shadow Blades (+1 flat damage — the frequent-attacker's pick, per the Heavenly Blades lesson) · Reality Wardens (+50% damage vs. summoned creatures — doubled if hero knows Nightshade Magic) · Firmness (−15% enemy Defense)

Reading the design from this tree: Advanced picks are playstyle forks (ranged vs. Focus economy vs. morale builds), Expert picks are meta answers — Reality Wardens at an effective +100% vs. summons is the skill-tree half of the anti-Schism/anti-summon toolkit this book keeps flagging.

docs.title: The Defense tree (verified fragments)
docs.text: Basic −10% from basic attacks → Advanced −15%, with documented subskill options including −20% vs. Ranged/Long Reach, +1 Focus Point when taking damage (doubled if hero knows Recruitment — the tank-battery build), and +2 Defense per point of Luck → Expert −20%, with options including −1 flat enemy damage and −10% Magic Damage (doubled if hero knows Diplomacy).

docs.title: Other verified subskill highlights
docs.text:
• Leadership/Luck: flat hero Morale (+1/+2 documented) and the capstones Resolve (+1% extra-turn chance per Morale point, doubled by Battlecraft) and Beastly Confidence (+1% Lucky Strike per Luck point, doubled by Primal Magic) — the engine of the 20%-per-morale-point stack documented in the combat appendix.
  • Tactics: widens your deployment zone (3 → 4 → 5 lines) and shrinks the enemy's if they also know Tactics — the only directly adversarial skill; also documented: +2 Attack per adjacent enemy (doubled by Offense) and a once-per-round +2 Initiative trigger when an enemy waits.
  • Resistance: includes a granted ability dealing heavy Magic Damage to a target summoned creature, and an effect stripping 1 level from all the enemy's Magic School spells — the anti-caster tree.
  • Sorcery-adjacent: a documented "+1 Focus Charge whenever the enemy casts" trigger — casters feeding your economy.

docs.title: Build guidance (original analysis)
docs.text:
1. Plan around the doublers. The synergy clauses are the skill system's real currency: Battle March + Luck, Resolve + Battlecraft, Reality Wardens + Nightshade, Beastly Confidence + Primal. A build that lines up two doublers outperforms a higher-level hero who rolled randomly.
  2. Might heroes: Offense/Defense core, Tactics into the adversarial clause vs. human-style opponents, Leadership for Living/Hive armies — and never morale skills for Undead or Construct armies (caps locked at 0; see the combat appendix).
  3. Magic heroes: your school skill is mandatory (it gates Tier 3–5 spells), Wisdom and Sorcery support it, Summon Avatar is the best free stats in the game (Neutral chapter, 4/5).
  4. Everyone: the subclass system (five specific Expert skills → subclass, per hero-class pages like the Herald's Progenitor) makes Expert-rank choices campaign-length commitments — check your target subclass's prerequisites before spending Expert picks.

docs.title: Open gaps (tracked)
docs.text:
The complete subskill text for every tree (Battlecraft, Siegecraft, Recruitment, Combat, Thaumaturgy, Wisdom, Battle Magic, Diplomacy, Logistics, Scouting, Insight, Economy in full) lives on the wiki's Skills Overview page; the trees above are the verified portion. Filling the remainder is a single-page fetch queued in the audit.

Sourcing note: system rules, skill list, and all quoted subskill effects from the official wiki's Skills and Skills Overview pages (retrieved 2026-07-03). Build guidance and tree readings are original analysis.

docs.title: How the system works (verified)
docs.text:
• Heroes carry 14 equipment slots (head, neck, torso, hands, main hand, waist, legs, feet, rings, and multiple misc-item slots — misc allows four at once). Bonuses apply only while equipped.
  • Four rarity categories; higher rarity = rarer and stronger. Relics sit at the top with unique effects — their signature pattern is granting every unlearned subskill of specific skill trees (bypassing the pick-one-of-three system entirely).
  • Artifacts can be upgraded with alchemical dust, improving their main bonus.
  • Sets: each artifact may belong to one. Minimal sets (2 pieces) add a bonus when both are worn; expanded sets (up to 8 pieces) unlock staged bonuses — typically at 3, 5, and 8 pieces.
  • Sources: found on the map, won from guards, or bought from an artifact merchant built in your city.

docs.title: Why sets matter (verified design intent)
docs.text:
Past hero level ~20, level-ups slow to a crawl — sets are the documented late-game growth axis, with full-set bonuses described as playstyle-transforming rather than incremental. Two verified flagships:

  • Knight's Honor (Plate Armor, Lance, Misericorde, Armet, Drum of War): +5% morale-trigger chance per point — a core piece of the 20%-per-morale-point stack documented in the combat appendix.
  • Power of the Dragon Father (8 pieces): full-set grants magic immunity — Armageddon-build enablement for factions without Black Dragons or Abyssal Envoys. Partial bonuses at 3/5 are modest; this set is all-or-nothing.
  • Tabar Signet-Ring + Ukh Signet (2-piece): Luck + Morale rings whose pairing adds a strong experience bonus — the cheapest set payoff in the game.

docs.title: The verified standouts, by slot
docs.text:
Head: Spellbinder's Hat (adds all Tier-5 spells to the spellbook — an Expert-school shortcut in an item) · Fancy Mask (+2→3 Focus Points at battle start — Murmuring in a hat) · Two-faced Mask (kill with Heroic Strike → use it again that round).

Main hand: Shroomwood Bow (removes the ranged penalty for nearby units, +10% ranged damage upgraded — a Marksman-line effect for the whole army) · Shaman Staff (+6→9 Spellpower — the biggest raw caster stat documented) · Caduceus (+4 mana per Heroic Strike use).

Torso: Vortex Robe (restores a large share of mana per spell cast — reported as 50% of cost, with an enemy-cast variant at 40→60%; the premier sustain item for casters either way).

Waist: Skull of Milos (+1,000 gold per day, Legendary — the earlier found, the more it prints) · Spells in Bottles (all Tier-4 spells).

Feet: Warlord Boots (+2 Initiative to all friendly units — turn order as an item) · Legion's Leg (+50% unit growth in all cities — the single biggest economy artifact documented; note the overlap with the Herald's Progenitor subclass for a truly absurd Hive stack).

Back/misc: Light-and-Shade Cloak (ranged attacks usable at close range — deletes the shooter's one weakness) · Fallen Angel Wings (flight over obstacles — Shadowflight, permanently) · Tranquil Glass (fully restores the hero's mana every day) · Quivering Quiver (ranged-army staple) · mana-pool misc items (+30 combined from the two standard picks).

Relics: the Holy Sigil of Roph is the documented archetype — all unlearned subskills of every faction skill (Righteousness, Necromancy, Murmuring, Triumvirate's Strength, Abyssal Communion, Summon Swarm). There is no "best relic" in general; each maps to a skill package, so the right one is whichever completes your build.

docs.title: Build packages (original analysis)
docs.text:
• The Caster Engine: Shaman Staff + Vortex Robe + Tranquil Glass
  • Fine Wand (+1 level to all spells) — Spellpower, sustain, and a free spell-level; every magic chapter's numbers scale up a bracket.
  • The Ranged Army Kit: Shroomwood Bow + Light-and-Shade Cloak + Quivering Quiver + Warlord Boots — removes every structural weakness this book's shooter-core advice carries.
  • The Economy Snowball: Skull of Milos + Legion's Leg — gold and growth; strongest in the earliest weeks you can assemble it.
  • The Morale Stack: Knight's Honor set + Ukh Signet + Resolve subskill + Encouragement law — the combat appendix's 20%-per-point ceiling, itemized.

docs.title: Open gaps (tracked)
docs.text:
The full artifact list with exact stats per rarity tier lives across the wiki's Artifacts page and the first-party database; this chapter documents the verified system rules, both flagship sets, and the cross-source consensus standouts. A complete item-by-item table is queued in the audit — and note two sources report Vortex Robe's trigger differently (own-cast 50% vs. enemy-cast 40→60%), flagged rather than resolved.

Sourcing note: system rules from the official wiki and first-party artifact database; item effects cross-checked across three contemporary guides (retrieved 2026-07-03). Build packages are original analysis.

docs.title: Neutral Creatures — the map's own army
How neutrals work (verified)
docs.text:
Faction units come in 7 tiers with dual upgrades; neutral creatures break both rules: they span 8 tiers, and cannot be upgraded — one version each. They don't belong to any town. Four documented ways to get them into your army:

  1. Specialized hiring buildings on the adventure map.
  2. Guard rewards — some defeated object-guards join you.
  3. The Chimerologist's Hut — rerolls native troops with a chance of neutral results (note: T8s don't drop from it).
  4. Summoning spells — temporary battle stacks (Starchild, Primal Remnant, and the Avatar are all technically neutral creatures; see their entries in the Nightshade, Primal, and Neutral Magic chapters).

Tier 8 is the headline: an elite band above every faction's ceiling, guarding the map's richest treasures. The documented example is the Lich Dragon, whose Bone Armageddon ability deals Pure Damage to all other creatures equal to its unit count — a 333-strong guard hits everything on the field for 333 unmodifiable damage. T8 fights are the game's raid bosses: bring answers, not armies.

docs.title: The verified roster (21 creatures)
docs.text:
Grouped by the role their identity and sourcing indicate; per-unit stat rows are the queued fetch noted in the audit.

Fodder & early-map guards: Pixie · Gnat · Peasant · Ghoul · Halfling — the week-1 XP diet. (Peasant is the series' traditional floor; Halfling its traditional ranged nuisance.)

Mid-map muscle: Animated Armor (Construct — morale/luck locked at 0, immune to those swings, and doesn't feed Necromancy) · Groll · Kittenhorn · Giant Toad · Unicorn.

Summon-only stacks: Starchild (Nightshade's Summon Starchild) · Primal Remnant (Primal's summon) · Avatar (the hero-scaled champion — the one neutral documented with full formulas, in the Neutral Magic chapter).

Elite guards & prizes: Faerie Dragon · Couatl · Dragonslayer · Worldwatcher · Sentinel of Glory.

The dragon tier: Obsidian Dragon · Red Dragon · Lich Dragon (T8) — the treasure-guard class, with the Lich Dragon's Bone Armageddon as the documented apex threat.

docs.title: Fighting neutrals well (original analysis)
docs.text:
• Read the type line before the stat line. The combat appendix's type table decides these fights: Constructs ignore your morale debuffs and feed Necromancy nothing; Undead neutrals (Ghoul, Lich Dragon) can't be Slept; Living/Magic Creature guards are Necromancy's richest harvest.
  • Percentage damage beats dragon guards. Guillotine (Arcane, 7 mana, % of max HP, stacking recasts) exists for exactly the high-HP single-stack guards this chapter describes — often cheaper than the army losses of fighting fair.
  • Bone Armageddon math: unmodifiable damage to everything means the counter isn't armor, it's stack count and body doubles — summons eat the same 333 as your real units, for free. A Starchild/Remnant screen turns the T8's signature move into wasted violence. (Reality Distortion's damage-deferral is the expert-tier alternative.)
  • Read Minds / Groundsight first. The intel spells (Nightshade T2, Primal T1) exist for neutral-fight selection; against 8 tiers of guards, knowing exact counts is worth more than any single combat spell.
  • Chimerologist gambling pairs with Primordial Chaos logic: reroll what you can't fight into what you can — but variance cuts both ways; don't reroll solved fights.

docs.title: Open gaps (tracked)
docs.text:
Per-unit stat rows for all 21 neutrals (the wiki's Neutral Units page uses the same table format as the faction pages — one fetch), plus tier assignments for the mid-list. The roster itself, the 8-tier structure, acquisition rules, and the Lich Dragon's ability are verified.

Sourcing note: roster, tier system, acquisition methods, and Bone Armageddon from the official wiki's Units navigation and the first-party unit database (retrieved 2026-07-03). Role groupings and tactical guidance are original analysis.

docs.title: Town Development — the other half of every turn
docs.text: Armies win battles; towns win campaigns. This chapter covers the verified building categories, the Laws system, and the build-order principles the faction chapters apply — plus the two faction-specific Week 1 plans this book has verified sourcing for.

docs.title: What a town builds (verified categories)
docs.text:
• Creature dwellings — one per tier, each unlocking that unit's weekly growth (the verified rates run from Skeletons' 30/week to T7s' 1–2/week). Upgraded dwellings unlock the dual upgrade paths documented in every faction's stat tables. External map dwellings supplement town growth (and are what Reinforcements/Relocation interact with remotely).
  • Mage Guild — unlocks spells for visiting heroes, and each guild built is worth 6 points toward your Neutral-spell auto-leveling (the single biggest points item in that system; see the Neutral Magic chapter). Ordinary buildings are worth 2 points each — every construction day quietly levels your logistics suite.
  • Marketplace — resource conversion at a loss; the verified framing from the Schism sourcing is "conversion fallback" for rare-resource bottlenecks, not a plan.
  • Artifact merchant — buyable artifacts (verified in the Artifacts chapter's acquisition list).
  • Fortifications — walls/towers/gate, the siege math from the Foundations chapter; the defender's half of "campaigns are decided at gates."
  • Faction-unique structures — e.g. Schism's Eerie Summoning Rite (the T7 Envoy building, verified on the Envoy's page).

docs.title: The Laws system (verified)
docs.text: Each faction has Laws — a parallel investment track upgraded with seals, separate from gold construction. Verified mechanics from the official Laws pages: each Law has 3 levels, each level improving only the numeric values of the same effect; in-game tooltips show your current benefit and the post-upgrade cumulative one. Laws are faction-flavored passives that reach outside the town — the one verified by name in this book is Encouragement (+2% morale-trigger chance per point), a required piece of the combat appendix's 20%-per-morale-point ceiling; and the Abyssal Envoy's page notes it is "specifically affected by" certain Schism Laws — Laws tune units, not just economy. Full per-faction Law tables: tracked as an open gap (six pages, same merged-tooltip format).

docs.title: Build-order principles (original analysis, anchored to verified plans)
docs.text:
The two verified Week 1 plans in this book:

Dungeon (from the pilot chapter): Day 1 Amphitheater → Days 2–3 Byzantine Palace (T2/T3 access) → Day 4 Labyrinth → Days 5–7 flex to map circumstances. The shape: income first, then the cheapest real-army tiers, then commit.

Schism (verified third-party build order): early army from T1–T2 dwellings with Cultist ranged damage → Days 3–4 split Mage Guild + Marketplace → Arbitrators (T6) dwelling accessible by the start of Week 2 → explicitly do not rush the T7 building ("puts too much pressure on the economy"), and when forced to choose between a building and recruiting, recruit.

The principles those two plans share — apply to any faction:

  1. Never skip a build day. Towns build one structure per day; a skipped day is unrecoverable. Even a "wrong" cheap building beats nothing (and pays 2 Neutral points).
  2. Dwellings before upgrades, breadth before height. Weekly growth starts accruing the day the dwelling exists — a dwelling built Day 2 instead of Day 5 is permanent extra army. Dual upgrades can wait; missed growth can't.
  3. Target a named Week 2 milestone. Every strong verified plan has one (Dungeon's Labyrinth line, Schism's Arbitrators, the Necropolis sourcing's "Dread Knight building by early Week 2," Temple's Lightweavers — or Zenith, who is the milestone, starting with them). Pick the faction chapter's flagged core unit and build the shortest path to it.
  4. The T7 building is a trap on schedule. Every faction's T7 is rare-resource gated (Foundations chapter); the building without the Gems/Mercury/Crystal to recruit from it is dead gold. Rush it only when the resource pipeline exists — which is what the economy-specialist second heroes are for.
  5. Recruit > build when the map is hot. Buildings compound but armies act; the verified Schism guidance generalizes — an unrecruited growth pool is the one town resource that doesn't carry forward safely (enemy heroes exist).
  6. Growth multipliers outrank everything late. The verified stack: Legion's Leg artifact (+50% growth, all cities) and the Herald's Progenitor subclass (+200%) turn each additional town into multiples — which is why the mid-game priority shifts from building your first town tall to taking your second and third.

docs.title: Multi-town play (original analysis)
docs.text: Once you hold two-plus towns, the verified logistics suite becomes the actual build order: Town Portal (garrison anywhere), Relocation (collect growth remotely), Assemble!/Gate of Light (feed the front). The town-development decision stops being "what do I build" and becomes "which town builds army, which builds economy" — army towns want dwellings and fortifications; economy towns want the Mage Guild (points + spells), Marketplace, and merchant. Your capital should usually be the army town: it's the one you'll defend.

docs.title: Open gaps (tracked)
docs.text:
Per-faction building trees with costs/prerequisites (six pages); full Law tables per faction (six pages); town-hall income tiers; weekly event effects on growth. The principles above are anchored to verified plans; the exact numbers behind them are the queued fetches.

Sourcing note: building categories, Neutral-points values, Laws mechanics, and both Week 1 plans from the verified sources cited in the faction and magic chapters (retrieved 2026-07-03). The six principles and multi-town framing are original analysis.

docs.title: Decision Trees — the book, compressed into flowcharts
docs.text: Every tree here is a compression of chapters you've already read (or can skip to). They're built for the moment of decision, not for study — the prose after each one tells you which chapter to open when the compressed answer isn't enough. All diagrams are original to this book.

docs.title: Tree 1: Which faction should I play?
docs.text: The tree's first question — what do you want wins to feel like? — is doing real work: the six factions genuinely divide into three temperaments. Safe & steady (Temple's buffed line, Grove's penalty-free shooters), inevitable (Necropolis's harvest, Schism's streaks), fast & sharp (Dungeon's precision, Hive's swarm). The two orange boxes are honest warnings, not insults: Schism and Hive carry the book's 2/5 beginner-friendliness scores, and both chapters explain exactly why. The bottom check is the Matchup Grid's clock rule — tempo picks are on a timer against the inevitability picks. Full reasoning: the six faction chapters' verdicts.

docs.title: Tree 2: Should I take this fight?
docs.text: The most expensive mistakes in a campaign are fights you shouldn't have taken — and fights you shouldn't have skipped. The tree encodes the Foundations chapter's Week 1 doctrine: intel first (Read Minds costs 7 mana; a wrong guess costs stacks), zero-loss fights are always taken (XP compounds), and losses are only acceptable when they buy compounding assets — a mine, an artifact, a dwelling — and the army regrows this week. The "wait one growth week" box is the most under-used button in the genre: the same fight is cheaper next Monday. Necropolis players: your tree tilts hard toward "take it" — fights are your economy (faction chapter).

docs.title: Tree 3: What should my hero cast this round?
docs.text: Eighty-three spells reduce to five questions, in strict priority order: protect (a fight-losing hit outranks everything — Carapace for one big hit, Taunt/Radiant Armour for many), amplify (if the army attacks a target this round, Vulnerability comes first — the sequencing lesson the Nightshade chapter repeats), control (one problem stack → remove it from the game, after checking the immunity lists), area (2+ adjacent stacks → the AoE suite; Black Hole manufactures its own clusters), and only then default single-target value. "Bank mana + Heroic Strike" is a legitimate answer — the Foundations chapter's free-spell framing.

docs.title: Tree 4 & 5: the Dungeon pilot's originals
docs.text: The project's first two diagrams, from the Dungeon pilot chapter — kept here so all the book's trees live in one place. Their faction-agnostic lessons graduated into the Town Development chapter's six principles (income → cheap tiers → milestone → flex) and the faction chapters' army-composition advice (one dominant stack, screened).

docs.title: How to read any tree in this book
docs.text:
1. Trees encode priority order, not exhaustive options — when two boxes both apply, the higher one wins.
  2. Every leaf has a chapter behind it; the tree is the index, the chapter is the argument.
  3. Trees are the most patch-fragile content in the book: they compress verdicts, and verdicts move. Check the chapter's patch_verified date before trusting a leaf in a tournament.

Sourcing note: all five diagrams are original SVGs created for this book (no game assets used); the decision logic compresses the verified analysis of the chapters referenced above.

docs.title: Campaign Play — the long game
docs.text: This book rates everything ⚔️ for campaign because campaign is where the game's systems compound longest. This chapter collects the campaign-specific doctrine the other chapters built, plus what's verified about the setting. What it deliberately doesn't contain: mission-by-mission walkthroughs — the Early Access campaign is still growing, no reliable mission sourcing meets this book's standard yet, and inventing walkthroughs is exactly what the audits exist to prevent. Tracked as the category's open gap.

docs.title: The world you're fighting over (verified lore)
docs.text: The setting is Jadame, and the six factions' verified blurbs sketch the war: the Church of the Sun's High Inquisition (Temple) in renewed conflict with its old enemy Shadowspire, home of the Necromancers' Guild (Necropolis); the Triumvirate of Alvar — "formerly underground, currently backstage rulers of Jadame" (Dungeon); a splinter cult of Alvari elves in the Frozen Vast who tore open a Rift after Lyssara's failed expedition (Schism — and she "insists they are under her control," per the Envoy's own page, unconvincingly); Beelzebub's Order of the Fly under the Dragonfly King, "bent on conquering Jadame" (Hive); and the Murmurwoods, whose governing body is officially Unknown (Grove). Two verified character threads worth watching for: the twinned sisters Deira (Dungeon) and Keiri (Schism), and campaign-obtainable artifacts — the Fine Wand (+1 level to all spells) is confirmed as a campaign mission reward.

docs.title: The five doctrines (the book's campaign advice, consolidated)
docs.text:
1. Zero-loss is the win condition inside the win condition. Campaigns are sequences; losses in mission N are weakness in mission N+1. The verified toolkit, in escalating order: fight selection (Tree 2), range + control (Web/Twilight), summons as ammunition (Starchild, Larvae, Communion units — free deaths), healing (Healing Water at 6 mana), and the crown — Rewind Life, the book's only 5/5 campaign spell, because it makes losses reversible.

2. Compounding picks beat peak picks. A campaign hero levels for dozens of hours: Pip's +1 attribute per 2 levels, school-masters' per-10-level scaling, specialists' per-2-level spell bonuses, and economy artifacts found early (Skull of Milos) all out-earn flashier immediate power. The heroes chapter's archetype guidance doubles in weight here.

3. The weekly rhythm is the real difficulty setting. Never skip a build day, never leave growth unrecruited before a hot border, never end a week without spending the logistics suite (Town Portal / Relocation are rated 5/5 and 4/5 for this mode). Campaign difficulty is mostly self-inflicted tempo loss.

4. Learn the counter-tech before you need it. The matchup grid's recurring swing cards — Fatal Decay (4 mana, switches off entire sustain factions), anti-summon effects (Heavenly Blades L4, Anti-Magic), Wean against buff-stackers — are cheap to carry and mission-deciding when the campaign throws the matching enemy at you. Campaign enemies telegraph their faction; read the grid's row before the mission, not during.

5. Bank flexibility for the unknown. Early Access campaigns change under your feet (Schism's post-Patch-8 re-tuning is the verified example). Favor heroes and builds this book rates for breadth — school-masters, the four-currency conversion framing from Foundations — over single-trick builds a patch can delete.

docs.title: Difficulty & mode notes (original analysis, flagged as such)
docs.text: Where a campaign offers hero carryover between missions, doctrine 2 becomes doctrine zero — every level and artifact is permanent infrastructure, and slow-rolling a won mission to over-level is legitimate play. Where it doesn't, doctrine 3 dominates: fresh starts reward the players with the cleanest Week 1s. The book's faction ⚔️ scores already assume campaign context; for a first campaign, the introduction's reading order stands — Temple, then branch out.

docs.title: Open gaps (tracked)
docs.text:
Mission-by-mission walkthroughs and scripted-event documentation (awaiting stable sourcing as the Early Access campaign expands); carryover rules per campaign; difficulty-modifier specifics.

Sourcing note: all lore and named details verified against the official pages cited in Parts I–III; the five doctrines consolidate this book's own verified analysis; the difficulty section is original analysis and labeled as such.

docs.title: Appendix: The Combat Math Under Everything
docs.text: The formulas this book's battle examples simplify away, verified from first-party stat documentation. This is the page to consult when you want your exact numbers instead of the reference-stack approximations.

docs.title: The damage pipeline
docs.text:
• Base damage: the unit's damage roll × stack size.
  • Attack multiplier: damage × (1 + 5% × attacker's Attack).
  • Defense reduction: result ÷ (1 + 5% × defender's Defense).
  • Practical shorthand (used throughout this book): each point of Attack-over-Defense shifts the exchange ~5%.

docs.title: Attack types
docs.text:
• Melee: adjacent targets only; provokes counterattacks.
  • Ranged: any distance; −10% damage per hex beyond 3 (floor −50%); forced into a −50% melee swing when adjacent. Units flagged "no range penalty" ignore the falloff; "no melee penalty" keep full damage adjacent; "no penalties" ignore both.
  • Long Reach: hits the hex behind an adjacent hex — no counterattack provoked, no movement onto the target.

docs.title: Turn economy
docs.text:
• Initiative: turn order within the round (Wait shifts the queue).
  • Speed: movement range in hexes.
  • Morale: +4% chance per point of an extra turn (negative: chance to skip). Luck: +6% chance per point of a Lucky Strike (bonus damage).
  • Native terrain: +1 Initiative for every faction on home ground (Temple: Grass · Necropolis: Deathland · Grove: Autumn · Hive: Lava · Schism: Snow · Dungeon: Dirt); each faction skill's terrain subskill adds +10% dealt / −10% taken.

docs.title: Morale/Luck caps by creature type
docs.text:
Each unit type carries its own range — and its Necromancy interaction:

Type Morale range Luck range Feeds Necromancy? Living −5…5 −3…3 ✅ Yes Hive Spawn −5…5 −5…5 ✅ Yes Magic Creature −3…3 −5…5 ✅ Yes Dragon 0…5 0…3 ✅ Yes Embodiment −3…3 −3…3 ❌ No Undead always 0 −5…5 ❌ No Construct always 0 always 0 ❌ No

Two campaign-shaping consequences: Necromancy harvests almost everything except mirror-Necropolis, Angels-and-spirits, and constructs — and morale/luck investment is wasted on Undead and Construct armies (their caps are locked at zero).

docs.title: Stacking morale — the documented ceiling
docs.text: The per-point trigger chance itself can be boosted: hero specialists (Aeos the Exalted reaches +7% at level 20), the Knight's Honor artifact set (+5%), the Resolve subskill (+2%, itself doubled by knowing Battlecraft), and the Encouragement law (+2%) stack to 20% per morale point — at which a morale-5 Living stack triggers extra turns every single round. The deepest documented "build-around" in the game's math.

docs.title: Focus economy
docs.text:
Creatures earn Focus Points by attacking and being attacked; banked points power their active abilities. Levers documented in this book: Murmuring (Grove: 1–3 free charges at battle start), Energize/Energy Explosion (Arcane), Battle March and kin (Offense-tree subskills), Silence (Nightshade: the off-switch).

Sourcing note: formulas and type tables from first-party stat documentation (olden-era.com unit database, retrieved 2026-07-03), cross-consistent with official wiki unit pages. Consequences and shorthand are original analysis.
